EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure over Minnesota and Wisconsin brought a prolonged period of  very strong winds to much of eastern Nebraska.  Especially hard hit was an area from around Omaha west through Columbus and then north through Albion and Norfolk.  Wind gusts of 58 to 63 mph were measured across much of that area, with a prolonged period of sustained winds of 35 to 43 mph observed from around Columbus north and northwest through Albion and Norfolk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 35 to 43 mph and a few gusts of around 60 mph prevailed for several hours across the county based on observations at surrounding sites.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 35 to 43 mph and with a peak gust of 61 mph was recorded by the Awos at Albion.  The winds caused several fires to burn out of control around the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued over extreme northeast Nebraska in October with below normal rainfall keeping soil and vegetation dry. Harvest of drought affected crops was completed, but there was no estimation available on how much yields were reduced. Winter wheat was planted on time, but the lack of moisture greatly hampered germination. Water restrictions were generally eased, with water use dropping off with the fall season. Drought was generally listed as continued severe to extreme for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued to aggravate existing long term dry soil conditions. The fall harvest was generally completed with definitely reduced yields, but no estimation on how much the reduction in yields was.  The dryness hampered the germination of winter wheat which was planted. The effect of local water use restrictions eased with the dropping off of water usage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph were observed for a few hours in Douglas county with a peak gust of 62 mph at the NWS office near Valley and 64 mph at Eppley Airfield.  The winds caused scattered tree damage in town.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph and a few gusts near 55 mph caused scattered tree damage across the county and especially in the Fremont area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure over Minnesota and Wisconsin brought a prolonged period of  very strong winds to much of eastern Nebraska.  Especially hard hit was an area from around Omaha west through Columbus and then north through Albion and Norfolk.  Wind gusts of 58 to 63 mph were measured across much of that area, with a prolonged period of sustained winds of 35 to 43 mph observed from around Columbus north and northwest through Albion and Norfolk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 35 to 43 mph and gusts of 50 to 60 mph, based on observations at surrounding sites, likely buffeted much Butler county.  These strong winds caused a picnic shelter to collapse in David City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced damaging winds in southern Dixon County on the evening of August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including up to 6 inch diameter limbs broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued over extreme northeast Nebraska in October with below normal rainfall keeping soil and vegetation dry. Harvest of drought affected crops was completed, but there was no estimation available on how much yields were reduced. Winter wheat was planted on time, but the lack of moisture greatly hampered germination. Water restrictions were generally eased, with water use dropping off with the fall season. Drought was generally listed as continued severe to extreme for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued to aggravate existing long term dry soil conditions. The fall harvest was generally completed with definitely reduced yields, but no estimation on how much the reduction in yields was.  The dryness hampered the germination of winter wheat which was planted. The effect of local water use restrictions eased with the dropping off of water usage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed along a cold front in eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the afternoon of June 10th.  Dew point temperatures in the upper 60s combined with temperatures around 90 to produce a modestly unstable airmass.  However, wind shear values were marginal and most of the activity was sub-severe.  A second area of thunderstorms developed behind the cold front in the early evening and these storms too produced some small hail and gusty winds.   Meanwhile later in the evening the cold front slowed and the storms along it trained across the same area producing areas of heavy rain in southwest Iowa.

Thunderstorms developed along an instability axis extending across eastern Nebraska. As increasing mid level lapse rates and stronger surface convergence increased ahead of a surface cold front, a few of the thunderstorms became severe.

A broken line of thunderstorms developed behind a slow moving cold front in eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the afternoon of June 20th.  Instability across the region was modestly high, but wind shear was marginal and thus the storms mainly produced areas of heavy rain.  However, one wet microburst occurred in the Omaha area causing tree and property damage, and training of heavy rain producing cells caused some flash flooding north of Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An apparent wet-microburst caused scattered tree and fence damage from around 130th to 144th Streets a little south of Harrison Street southwest of the Millard airport. Some of the fallen trees caused house, vehicle and light pole damage.  The winds were estimated to have been over 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an instability axis extending across eastern Nebraska. As increasing mid level lapse rates and stronger surface convergence increased ahead of a surface cold front, a few of the thunderstorms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1.50 inches was reported at 70th and A Street in Lincoln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms developed behind a slow moving cold front in eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the afternoon of June 20th.  Instability across the region was modestly high, but wind shear was marginal and thus the storms mainly produced areas of heavy rain.  However, one wet microburst occurred in the Omaha area causing tree and property damage, and training of heavy rain producing cells caused some flash flooding north of Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall estimated at around 3 inches in several hours caused flash flooding across parts of Highway 75 near Decatur for a few hours.  Damage was apparently minimal, but the flooding did detour traffic for a while.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed on a cold front that pressed across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the evening of August 15th. The storms produced a few reports of large hail.
Isolated severe thunderstorms developed on a cold front that pressed across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the evening of August 15th. The storms produced a few reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near 156th and Military.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed on a cold front that pressed across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the evening of August 15th. The storms produced a few reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail was reported near 72nd and Highway 36.  The hail was accompanied by winds estimated at 40 to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed on a cold front that pressed across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the evening of August 15th. The storms produced a few reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to nickel-size was reported near 35th and Farnam Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed on a cold front that pressed across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the evening of August 15th. The storms produced a few reports of large hail.
Isolated severe thunderstorms developed on a cold front that pressed across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the evening of August 15th. The storms produced a few reports of large hail.

Drought conditions continued extreme northeast Nebraska with well below normal rainfall keeping soil and vegetation dry. Rainfall for the month was less than half of normal. Stress on crops that prevailed over the growing season became even more evident with the start of harvest, although the amount of the reduced yields were still uncertain. Local governments continued to use water use restrictions in an effort to prevent serious water supply problems. Drought was generally listed as continued severe to extreme for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well below normal rainfall continued to aggravate existing long term dry soil conditions. Stress on crops which was observed during most of the summer became more evident with the beginning of harvest, although the amount of yield reduction and other damage was still uncertain. Corn, soybeans, and other crops were known to be severely affected, although corn continued to be the crop suspected of suffering the most damage. The drought continued to result in local water use restrictions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gradient winds in the wake of a thunderstorm complex caused scattered damage across parts of eastern Nebraska during the early morning hours of June 23rd.  The winds lasted for about an hour or so with sustained speeds of 30 mph or more and some gusts of 50 mph or more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds in the wake of a thunderstorm complex caused scattered tree, especially in the Endicott area.  The winds lasted for about an hour and were estimated at between 40 and 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced hail up to one inch.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued through October as below normal precipitation was observed. For the month, rainfall was near 1 inch below normal, while the yearly precipitation deficit was in excess of 9 inches. The exceptional (D4) drought continued into November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of high heat and humidity persisted for several days. Daytime temperatures reached the 90s to several degrees above 100, and dew points were in the 70s. The heat index went as high as 110 degrees. Low temperatures were in the 70s, leading to some uncooled indoor locations remaining excessively warm through the night. The dangerous nature of the heat was added to by its continuing over a period of several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures reaching the 90s to a few degrees above 100, and high humidity with dew points in the 70s, caused an extended period of excessive heat. The heat index peaked at 100 to 110 degrees each day, and did not fall enough at night to provide much relief to indoor locations that were not otherwise cooled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced strong outflow winds on July 21st. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure that brought several tornadoes to southeast Nebraska on April 14th, tracked across northeast Nebraska during the morning and early afternoon of April 15th.  Although a front had swept through the region, there was still enough instability around to allow for the development of low-topped mini super-cell thunderstorms that spawned a couple of brief weak tornadoes under the upper low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down in open field and caused no apparent damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was mostly pea-size but a few up to penny-size were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong east to southeast winds developed as high pressure over the Great Lakes region was replaced by low pressure that pressed east of the Rocky Mountains.  The strong winds caused isolated wind damage north of Omaha.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong east to southeast winds developed over the area during the morning and afternoon of April 27.  The strong winds blew part of the roof off of a local business near Tekamah.  Winds were measured at around 45 mph by the Asos near Tekamah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms, a few severe with large hail and damaging winds, developed during the late afternoon and evening along a weak cool front that stretched from east central Nebraska into southwest Iowa.  Instability was modestly high as temperatures near the front climbed to around 100 degrees with dew point temperatures in the low to mid 60s.  However, deep wind shear and convergence near the boundary was weak which limited the overall coverage of the storms and any associated severe weather.

Thursday evening high based thunderstorms produced lightning that ignited four wildfires in Keith County and one in Arthur County. The wildfires burned about 60,000 acres of mainly grasslands, but also threatened dozens of summer houses, town houses, condominiums and rental cabins near the Bayside Golf Club on the southwest shore of Lake McConaughy during the Eagle Canyon Hideaway Fire. One wildfire in northeast Keith County spread into southwest McPherson County. The wildfires were contained overnight where loss of grasslands for grazing could exceed 500 thousand dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire was ignited by a lightning strike in Keith County at the Glen Echo area that spread into southwest McPherson County overnight. The wildfire burned an estimated 7,500 acres of grassland and timber before containment. Areas of canyon and creek valley were inaccessible for ground firefighting. Rough terrain prevented full containment, but two National Guard UH-60 Black Hawk helicopters provided air support that brought the fire under control before containment.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was accompanied by thunderstorm wind gusts which were estimated at around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms, a few severe, developed on a weak boundary during the afternoon of August 8th.  The boundary extended nearly north to south from east of Norfolk to east of Lincoln and Beatrice its position aided by earlier convection that tracked across northeast Nebraska and western Iowa.  Temperatures west of the boundary climbed to around 100 degrees with west to northwest winds.  Meanwhile, readings east of it were several degrees cooler with southeast winds.  The storms were further strengthened by an upper level disturbance that tracked southeast out of South Dakota which pushed a cool front south across the region.  The storms developed along the north to south boundary in Nebraska and produced pockets of strong winds and hail, some of it large, as they pushed into far southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1 inch in diameter covered the ground near Cook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures in June and July across eastern Colorado, and drier than normal conditions, have resulted in the area being rated in Extreme (D3) Drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened to Extreme Drought during July as a result of below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued through October as below normal precipitation was observed across western and north central Nebraska. All areas were at D4 or exceptional drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued through October as below normal precipitation was observed. For the month, rainfall was near 1 inch below normal, while the yearly precipitation deficit was in excess of 9 inches. The exceptional (D4) drought continued into November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms, a few severe, developed on a weak boundary during the afternoon of August 8th.  The boundary extended nearly north to south from east of Norfolk to east of Lincoln and Beatrice its position aided by earlier convection that tracked across northeast Nebraska and western Iowa.  Temperatures west of the boundary climbed to around 100 degrees with west to northwest winds.  Meanwhile, readings east of it were several degrees cooler with southeast winds.  The storms were further strengthened by an upper level disturbance that tracked southeast out of South Dakota which pushed a cool front south across the region.  The storms developed along the north to south boundary in Nebraska and produced pockets of strong winds and hail, some of it large, as they pushed into far southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow high-based funnel cloud eventually touched down in between Dodge and North Bend.  The tornado was very narrow and most likely caused little or no damage.  The funnel and/or tornado were observed and photographed from many different directions and from miles away.  The funnel initially formed on a developing towering cumulus cloud on a boundary in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures reaching the 90s and high humidity with dew points in the 70s caused excessive heat during the afternoon of August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures warmed into the 90s while dew points were in the 70s, resulting in heat index readings reaching the 100 to 105 degree range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and very dry conditions persisted into early September across eastern Nebraska, as the first few days of the month saw little or no rainfall along with high temperatures frequently in the 90s.  This pushed the drought intensity into the Exceptional range in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender.  Temperatures moderated somewhat during the middle and end of September.  A mid-month rain event dropped around 1 inch to as much as 3 inches of rain near and southeast of Interstate 80, but amounts in northeast Nebraska were generally a half inch or less. That rain event was generally it for significant rainfall and rain totals for the month as a whole were around 2 inches below normal in northeast Nebraska and an inch or inch and half below normal elsewhere.  Thus the drought intensity remained in the exceptional category in northeast Nebraska and in the Extreme category over much of the rest of eastern Nebraska through September.  The exception was the far southeast corner of the state where the drought intensity moderated slightly into the severe category.

The drought that began late June continued to intensify as the trend of strong high pressure aloft sustained dry and hot weather. Temperatures were above average and the suppressed rainfall let to spotty rainfall in some areas. The Drought Monitor continued to reflect short-term impacts, while a large portion of the sandhills showed the drought exceptional (D4), otherwise extreme drought conditions lingers for the region. Record low flows on streams have increased with an exception of the North Platte River below Kingsley Dam, where higher flows were maintained for irrigation needs. The fire dangers were elevated through the month. High based thunderstorms brought an increase risk of fire starts. The drought continued into September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began late June continued to into August and intensified. The drought conditions remained extreme (D3) except for the southwest where the drought intensified to exceptional (D4) drought conditions. The weather pattern trended toward strong high pressure aloft that sustained dry and hot weather. Temperatures were above average and the suppressed rainfall led to spotty rainfall at best. The Drought Monitor continued to reflect short-term impacts, while the drought intensified into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in less than a week, a severe thunderstorm impacted western portions of Dawson County. However, this Wednesday evening event was more significant as wind gusts estimated at 60 MPH combined with quarter to golf ball size hail to damage crops, especially along a swath from near Cozad to several miles east of Cozad. The storm gradually weakened as it entered the eastern half of the county, with wind gusts at the Lexington airport topping out at 52 MPH. Although Dawson County bore the brunt of severe weather mainly between 730-830 PM CDT, a smattering of strong thunderstorms also clipped parts of western Sherman and extreme northwestern Buffalo Counties within South Central Nebraska. The first strong storms in Dawson County initially flared up between 5-6 PM CDT, but by 9 PM CDT all storms within the area had either faded away or weakened considerably. The primary severe storm that affected Cozad and points east was part of a broken, southwest-to-northeast oriented line of multicell storms stretched along an axis from eastern Custer County Nebraska to Rawlins and Decatur Counties in northwest Kansas. Although initial storm development was not tied to a well-defined surface boundary and synoptic scale forcing aloft was weak due to the area being positioned just northeast of a mid-level ridge centered over Colorado, severe weather was able to develop within a zone of steep low-level lapse rates featuring surface temperatures in the mid-upper 90s and along a moisture gradient that separated surface dewpoints in the 50s to the west from 60s to the east. Around the time of the golf ball size hail near Cozad, mesoscale severe weather parameters advertised no more than 20-30 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear, but 1000-2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of golf balls resulted in heavy damage to nearby soybean and corn fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although the coverage of strong to severe thunderstorms was rather limited within the 24-county area on this Wednesday afternoon and evening, a small but intense, slow-moving supercell dumped quarter to golf ball size on a localized area near the Howard-Hall-Merrick County intersection between 4-5 PM CDT. Most of this hail fell in a swath between St. Libory and a few miles west of Chapman, and an observer a few miles east-northeast of St. Libory also reported a quick 1.82 inches of rain. As rapidly as this storm formed, it had all but dissipated by 530 PM CDT and ended up being the only storm of the day to produce confirmed severe weather in South Central Nebraska. However, between 430 PM and 6 PM CDT, a few other isolated strong storms briefly flared up in Nance, northern Polk and southwest Furnas Counties. Later in the evening, mainly between 9 PM and midnight CDT, another round of strong storms affected primarily Nuckolls, Fillmore and Thayer Counties in southeast portions of the area before exiting into Kansas. In the mid and upper levels the flow was quasi-zonal, with the primary synoptic forcing for this event consisting of subtle shortwave energy passing through the Central Plains along the southern periphery of a dominant upper low centered near the North Dakota-Manitoba border. At the surface, a slow moving cold front gradually passed through South Central Nebraska during the day, with the limited storm development focused on the immediate cool side of the boundary where dewpoints had pooled well into the 60s to near 70F. Around the time of the large hail near St. Libory, the mesoscale environment was quite unstable with around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE but with only around 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported in the area.

The drought that began late June continued to intensify as the trend of strong high pressure aloft sustained dry and hot weather. Temperatures were above average and the suppressed rainfall let to spotty rainfall in some areas. The Drought Monitor continued to reflect short-term impacts, while a large portion of the sandhills showed the drought exceptional (D4), otherwise extreme drought conditions lingers for the region. Record low flows on streams have increased with an exception of the North Platte River below Kingsley Dam, where higher flows were maintained for irrigation needs. The fire dangers were elevated through the month. High based thunderstorms brought an increase risk of fire starts. The drought continued into September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began late June continued to into August where extreme (D3) drought lingered and intensified to exceptional (D4) drought conditions in the south. The weather pattern trended toward strong high pressure aloft that sustained dry and hot weather. Temperatures were above average and the suppressed rainfall led to spotty rainfall at best. The Drought Monitor continued to reflect short-term impacts, while the drought intensified into September.

For Adams, Clay, York and Fillmore Counties, July marked a steady degradation into drought conditions. The entire area started out the month with no worse than category D1 moderate drought according to the U.S. Drought Monitor, but by month's end Adams County found itself in Category D3 extreme drought, while the other three counties were primarily in category D2 severe drought for the first time since 2006. This rapid onset of severe to extreme drought marked a downward spiral in drought conditions since early June, when the entire area was void of all drought categories. Looking back a few months to April and May, there was little hint of impending drought as two-month rainfall totals within most of the area averaged near normal to even above normal. In fact, much of Clay and Fillmore Counties were soaked with 4-6 inches of rain in May alone. Unfortunately, a major weather pattern change to a dominant high pressure ridge in June and July resulted in considerably less rainfall. Although these four counties generally fared better than northern and western portions of South Central Nebraska, total June-July rainfall across most of the area was no more than 25-60 percent of normal. This included York, where the two-month total of 2.01 was only 25 percent of the normal amount of 8.17. Other below normal June-July combined rainfall totals within these four counties included 3.99 at Hastings (53 percent of normal) and 4.67 at Geneva (59 percent of normal). For most of the area, July was notably drier than June, with a few of the lowest July totals including 0.50 at York and 0.62 at Geneva. By far the most fortunate county within all of South Central Nebraska during June and July was Clay County, which largely defied the building regional drought by registering near-normal to slightly above normal rainfall. However, even in those areas that saw more rainfall than others, crops were still stressed by heat. Average daily high temperatures for the combined months of June and July registered at least 5-7 degrees above normal, essentially baking maturing crops across much of the area. At Hastings airport, the average June-July daily high temperature was 90.4 degrees, or 5.1 degrees hotter than the 30-year normal. Between July 22nd-24th, Hastings airport reached at least 100 degrees three days in row for the first time 2005. For the week ending July 29th, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that some corn was being chopped for silage or cut for hay to compensate for poor pasture conditions. In addition, state-averaged topsoil and subsoil moisture was indicated as 96 percent short to very short. Poor to very poor crop conditions were reported as 37 percent for corn, 38 percent for soybeans and 69 percent for alfalfa, and pasture and range conditions were rated as 83 percent poor to very poor.

For a short time on this early Thursday evening, mainly between 6-7 PM CDT, a lone outflow-dominant severe storm tracked from northwest to southeast across western Dawson County, resulting in one report of estimated 60 MPH winds northwest of Cozad. This storm first entered Dawson County north of Gothenburg, and after producing the strong winds in the Cozad area it quickly weakened before reaching the Gosper County line. This was the only storm to become severe within South Central Nebraska as a broken, southwest-to-northeast oriented line of high-based multicell convection sagged into the area from the northwest along an axis from Valley and Greeley Counties to Dawson County. This broken line of storms initially developed well northwest of the local area during the mid-afternoon near a surface trough draped from north central to west central Nebraska. On the larger scale aloft, these storms occurred within a modest northwesterly mid-level flow regime, with the area positioned between a southwestern United States ridge and a shortwave trough centered near the North Dakota-Minnesota border. Despite no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear, the presence of around 1000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE in an environment characterized by steep low level lapse rates with surface temperatures in the low-mid 90s was enough to promote this severe storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 mph were reported.

For the second time in less than a week, a severe thunderstorm impacted western portions of Dawson County. However, this Wednesday evening event was more significant as wind gusts estimated at 60 MPH combined with quarter to golf ball size hail to damage crops, especially along a swath from near Cozad to several miles east of Cozad. The storm gradually weakened as it entered the eastern half of the county, with wind gusts at the Lexington airport topping out at 52 MPH. Although Dawson County bore the brunt of severe weather mainly between 730-830 PM CDT, a smattering of strong thunderstorms also clipped parts of western Sherman and extreme northwestern Buffalo Counties within South Central Nebraska. The first strong storms in Dawson County initially flared up between 5-6 PM CDT, but by 9 PM CDT all storms within the area had either faded away or weakened considerably. The primary severe storm that affected Cozad and points east was part of a broken, southwest-to-northeast oriented line of multicell storms stretched along an axis from eastern Custer County Nebraska to Rawlins and Decatur Counties in northwest Kansas. Although initial storm development was not tied to a well-defined surface boundary and synoptic scale forcing aloft was weak due to the area being positioned just northeast of a mid-level ridge centered over Colorado, severe weather was able to develop within a zone of steep low-level lapse rates featuring surface temperatures in the mid-upper 90s and along a moisture gradient that separated surface dewpoints in the 50s to the west from 60s to the east. Around the time of the golf ball size hail near Cozad, mesoscale severe weather parameters advertised no more than 20-30 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear, but 1000-2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE.
A scattered line of thunderstorms produced large hail along an eastward moving cold front across the eastern half of north central Nebraska and a small portion of west central Nebraska.

For these 12 counties in South Central Nebraska, July marked a rapid degradation in drought conditions. The entire area started out the month with no worse than category D1 moderate drought according to the U.S. Drought Monitor, but by month's end most of the area found itself in category D3 extreme drought for the first time since at least 2006 with the exception of Nuckolls and Thayer Counties, which ended the month in category D2 severe drought. As of July 31st, all counties except Nuckolls were either a primary or contiguous county in a federally-declared drought disaster area. This rapid onset of severe to extreme drought marked a downward spiral in drought conditions since early June, when the entire area was still free of all drought categories. Looking back a few months to April and May, although some areas near the Kansas border and also west of Highway 281 started to dry out quite a bit in May, the majority of these 12 counties averaged fairly close to normal rainfall for these two months. As a result, June and July would prove critical as to whether substantial drought would develop. Unfortunately, these two months yielded no more than 20-50 percent of normal precipitation across most of the area, or generally between 1.50-4.00 inches. Serving as one of many examples, the combined June-July rainfall total of 1.43 at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island was only 19 percent of the normal amount of 7.70, and included the second-driest July on record with only 0.16. Other well-below normal June-July combined rainfall totals within these 12 counties included 0.96 at Ravenna, 1.54 at Osceola, 1.84 at Central City and 2.80 at Franklin. A wetter exception was focused in Nuckolls and Thayer Counties mainly due to a rather rainy June, which helped spare these counties from sinking to extreme drought in July. For most of the area, July was notably drier than June, and in fact observation sites in Polk County such as Osceola and 3 miles northeast of Shelby reported no more than a non-measurable trace of rain for the month. Area rivers were significantly impacted as well, including the Platte which essentially dried up from near Grand Island downstream to Columbus. As though the lack of rain was not enough to hasten the onset of drought, average daily high temperatures for the combined months of June and July registered at least 5-7 degrees above normal, essentially baking moisture-starved maturing crops. At Grand Island, the average June-July daily high temperature was 92.4 degrees, or 6.9 degrees hotter than the 30-year normal. Between July 21st-24th, Grand Island reached at least 100 degrees four days in row for the first time 2006. Closer to the Kansas border, Red Cloud reached at least 100 degrees 14 times in July. For the week ending July 29th, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that some corn was being chopped for silage or cut for hay to compensate for poor pasture conditions. In addition, state-averaged topsoil and subsoil moisture was indicated as 96 percent short to very short. Poor to very poor crop conditions were reported as 37 percent for corn, 38 percent for soybeans and 69 percent for alfalfa, and pasture and range conditions were rated as 83 percent poor to very poor.

Between July 21st-24th, Grand Island reached at least 100 degrees four days in row for the first time 2006. Closer to the Kansas border, Red Cloud reached at least 100 degrees 14 times in July. For the week ending July 29th, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that some corn was being chopped for silage or cut for hay to compensate for poor pasture conditions. In addition, state-averaged topsoil and subsoil moisture was indicated as 96 percent short to very short. Poor to very poor crop conditions were reported as 37 percent for corn, 38 percent for soybeans and 69 percent for alfalfa, and pasture and range conditions were rated as 83 percent poor to very poor.

For these 12 counties in South Central Nebraska, July marked a rapid degradation in drought conditions. The entire area started out the month with no worse than category D1 moderate drought according to the U.S. Drought Monitor, but by month's end most of the area found itself in category D3 extreme drought for the first time since at least 2006 with the exception of Nuckolls and Thayer Counties, which ended the month in category D2 severe drought. As of July 31st, all counties except Nuckolls were either a primary or contiguous county in a federally-declared drought disaster area. This rapid onset of severe to extreme drought marked a downward spiral in drought conditions since early June, when the entire area was still free of all drought categories. Looking back a few months to April and May, although some areas near the Kansas border and also west of Highway 281 started to dry out quite a bit in May, the majority of these 12 counties averaged fairly close to normal rainfall for these two months. As a result, June and July would prove critical as to whether substantial drought would develop. Unfortunately, these two months yielded no more than 20-50 percent of normal precipitation across most of the area, or generally between 1.50-4.00 inches. Serving as one of many examples, the combined June-July rainfall total of 1.43 at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island was only 19 percent of the normal amount of 7.70, and included the second-driest July on record with only 0.16. Other well-below normal June-July combined rainfall totals within these 12 counties included 0.96 at Ravenna, 1.54 at Osceola, 1.84 at Central City and 2.80 at Franklin. A wetter exception was focused in Nuckolls and Thayer Counties mainly due to a rather rainy June, which helped spare these counties from sinking to extreme drought in July. For most of the area, July was notably drier than June, and in fact observation sites in Polk County such as Osceola and 3 miles northeast of Shelby reported no more than a non-measurable trace of rain for the month. Area rivers were significantly impacted as well, including the Platte which essentially dried up from near Grand Island downstream to Columbus. For the second time in less than a week, a severe thunderstorm impacted western portions of Dawson County. However, this Wednesday evening event was more significant as wind gusts estimated at 60 MPH combined with quarter to golf ball size hail to damage crops, especially along a swath from near Cozad to several miles east of Cozad. The storm gradually weakened as it entered the eastern half of the county, with wind gusts at the Lexington airport topping out at 52 MPH. Although Dawson County bore the brunt of severe weather mainly between 730-830 PM CDT, a smattering of strong thunderstorms also clipped parts of western Sherman and extreme northwestern Buffalo Counties within South Central Nebraska. The first strong storms in Dawson County initially flared up between 5-6 PM CDT, but by 9 PM CDT all storms within the area had either faded away or weakened considerably. The primary severe storm that affected Cozad and points east was part of a broken, southwest-to-northeast oriented line of multicell storms stretched along an axis from eastern Custer County Nebraska to Rawlins and Decatur Counties in northwest Kansas. Although initial storm development was not tied to a well-defined surface boundary and synoptic scale forcing aloft was weak due to the area being positioned just northeast of a mid-level ridge centered over Colorado, severe weather was able to develop within a zone of steep low-level lapse rates featuring surface temperatures in the mid-upper 90s and along a moisture gradient that separated surface dewpoints in the 50s to the west from 60s to the east. Around the time of the golf ball size hail near Cozad, mesoscale severe weather parameters advertised no more than 20-30 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear, but 1000-2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters resulted in heavy crop damage to nearby corn fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought that began late June continued to intensify as the trend of strong high pressure aloft sustained dry and hot weather. Temperatures were above average and the suppressed rainfall let to spotty rainfall in some areas. The Drought Monitor continued to reflect short-term impacts, while a large portion of the sandhills showed the drought exceptional (D4), otherwise extreme drought conditions lingers for the region. Record low flows on streams have increased with an exception of the North Platte River below Kingsley Dam, where higher flows were maintained for irrigation needs. The fire dangers were elevated through the month. High based thunderstorms brought an increase risk of fire starts. The drought continued into September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought that began late June continued to into August and intensified to an exceptional (D4) drought. The weather pattern trended toward strong high pressure aloft that sustained dry and hot weather. Temperatures were above average and the suppressed rainfall led to spotty rainfall at best. The Drought Monitor continued to reflect short-term impacts, while the drought intensified into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A scattered line of thunderstorms produced large hail along an eastward moving cold front across the eastern half of north central Nebraska and a small portion of west central Nebraska.

The entire area started out the month with no worse than category D1 moderate drought according to the U.S. Drought Monitor, but by month's end most of the area found itself in category D3 extreme drought for the first time since at least 2006 with the exception of Nuckolls and Thayer Counties, which ended the month in category D2 severe drought. As of July 31st, all counties except Nuckolls were either a primary or contiguous county in a federally-declared drought disaster area. This rapid onset of severe to extreme drought marked a downward spiral in drought conditions since early June, when the entire area was still free of all drought categories. Looking back a few months to April and May, although some areas near the Kansas border and also west of Highway 281 started to dry out quite a bit in May, the majority of these 12 counties averaged fairly close to normal rainfall for these two months. As a result, June and July would prove critical as to whether substantial drought would develop. Unfortunately, these two months yielded no more than 20-50 percent of normal precipitation across most of the area, or generally between 1.50-4.00 inches. Serving as one of many examples, the combined June-July rainfall total of 1.43 at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island was only 19 percent of the normal amount of 7.70, and included the second-driest July on record with only 0.16. Other well-below normal June-July combined rainfall totals within these 12 counties included 0.96 at Ravenna, 1.54 at Osceola, 1.84 at Central City and 2.80 at Franklin. A wetter exception was focused in Nuckolls and Thayer Counties mainly due to a rather rainy June, which helped spare these counties from sinking to extreme drought in July. For most of the area, July was notably drier than June, and in fact observation sites in Polk County such as Osceola and 3 miles northeast of Shelby reported no more than a non-measurable trace of rain for the month. Area rivers were significantly impacted as well, including the Platte which essentially dried up from near Grand Island downstream to Columbus. As though the lack of rain was not enough to hasten the onset of drought, average daily high temperatures for the combined months of June and July registered at least 5-7 degrees above normal, essentially baking moisture-starved maturing crops. At Grand Island, the average June-July daily high temperature was 92.4 degrees, or 6.9 degrees hotter than the 30-year normal. Between July 21st-24th, Grand Island reached at least 100 degrees four days in row for the first time 2006. Closer to the Kansas border, Red Cloud reached at least 100 degrees 14 times in July. For the week ending July 29th, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that some corn was being chopped for silage or cut for hay to compensate for poor pasture conditions. In addition, state-averaged topsoil and subsoil moisture was indicated as 96 percent short to very short. Poor to very poor crop conditions were reported as 37 percent for corn, 38 percent for soybeans and 69 percent for alfalfa, and pasture and range conditions were rated as 83 percent poor to very poor.

This area north of Interstate 80, including most of Valley, Greeley, Sherman and Howard Counties along with far western Nance County started out July in category D1 moderate drought according to the U.S. Drought Monitor, but by month's end had drastically degraded to category D4 exceptional drought for the first time since at least 2002. As of July 31st, all counties were either a primary or contiguous county in a federally-declared drought disaster area. This incredibly rapid onset of exceptional flash drought marked a downward spiral in drought conditions since early June, when the entire area was still free of all drought categories. In fact, precipitation during both April and May was slightly above normal across most of this area, including two-month totals of 7.55 at Ord and 6.83 at Greeley. However, a dramatic decrease in rainfall ensued during June and July with most of the area totaling less than 1 for the two months combined!  Serving as one of many examples, the combined June-July rainfall total of 0.48 at the Ord airport was merely 7 percent of the normal amount of 7.09, and was by far the driest June-July period on record. Other paltry June-July rainfall totals within the area included 0.38 at St. Paul, 0.62 at Greeley and 0.89 at Belgrade. Focusing solely on July, several places recorded no measurable rain during the month, including at Belgrade and near Dannebrog. As though the lack of rain was not enough to hasten the onset of drought, average daily high temperatures for the combined months of June and July registered at least 5-8 degrees above normal, essentially baking moisture-starved maturing crops. For the week ending July 29th, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that some corn was being chopped for silage or cut for hay to compensate for poor pasture conditions. In addition, state-averaged topsoil and subsoil moisture was indicated as 96 percent short to very short. Dawson, Gosper and Furnas Counties in far western portions of South Central Nebraska started out July in category D2 severe drought according to the U.S. Drought Monitor, but by month's end had degraded to category D3 extreme drought for the first time since at least 2006. As of July 31st, all three counties were either a primary or contiguous county in a federally-declared drought disaster area. This rapid onset of extreme drought represented a notable degradation in conditions since early May when the entire area was free of all drought categories. Looking back a few months prior to the onset of drought, a fairly wet April helped mitigate the impact of a rather dry March. As a result, the period from May into early June would prove critical as to whether drought would develop. Unfortunately, the combined months of May and June subsequently yielded no more than 25-45% of normal precipitation across most of the area. The situation continued to worsen in July, with much of this three-county area measuring less than 1 inch of rain during the month, versus the 30-year normal around 3.50 inches. A few of the lowest cooperative observer rainfall totals during July included 0.36 inches near Lexington, 0.65 inches in Gothenburg and 1.26 inches in Wilsonville. Despite the overall dryness, there were localized exceptions of heavier monthly rainfall, including Cambridge which stood out with 4.28 inches. However, the lack of rainfall was not the only factor contributing to the worsening drought. Average daily maximum temperatures for the combined months of June and July ended up well above normal, essentially baking moisture-starved maturing crops. In Gothenburg, the average June-July daily high temperature was 93.0 degrees, or 7.8 degrees hotter than the normal of 85.2 degrees. In Cambridge, ten July days reached at least 100 degrees, including five days in a row between the 19th-24th. For the week ending July 29th, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that some corn was being chopped for silage or cut for hay to compensate for poor pasture conditions. In addition, state-averaged topsoil and subsoil moisture was indicated as 96 percent short to very short. Poor to very poor crop conditions were reported as 37 percent for corn, 38 percent for soybeans and 69 percent for alfalfa, and pasture and range conditions were rated as 83 percent poor to very poor.

Hot and very dry conditions persisted into early September across eastern Nebraska, as the first few days of the month saw little or no rainfall along with high temperatures frequently in the 90s.  This pushed the drought intensity into the Exceptional range in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender.  Temperatures moderated somewhat during the middle and end of September.  A mid-month rain event dropped around 1 inch to as much as 3 inches of rain near and southeast of Interstate 80, but amounts in northeast Nebraska were generally a half inch or less. That rain event was generally it for significant rainfall and rain totals for the month as a whole were around 2 inches below normal in northeast Nebraska and an inch or inch and half below normal elsewhere.  Thus the drought intensity remained in the exceptional category in northeast Nebraska and in the Extreme category over much of the rest of eastern Nebraska through September.  The exception was the far southeast corner of the state where the drought intensity moderated slightly into the severe category.

Thursday evening high based thunderstorms produced lightning that ignited four wildfires in Keith County and one in Arthur County. The wildfires burned about 60,000 acres of mainly grasslands, but also threatened dozens of summer houses, town houses, condominiums and rental cabins near the Bayside Golf Club on the southwest shore of Lake McConaughy during the Eagle Canyon Hideaway Fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire at the Eagle Canyon Hideaway that burned up to 3,000 acres of grassland and timber.

The wildfires burned about 60,000 acres of mainly grasslands, but also threatened dozens of summer houses, town houses, condominiums and rental cabins near the Bayside Golf Club on the southwest shore of Lake McConaughy during the Eagle Canyon Hideaway Fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire at the Glen Echo area that burned an estimated 7,500 acres of grassland and timber. Areas of canyon and creek valley were inaccessible for ground firefighting. The wildfire moved into southwest McPherson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms in the southeast Nebraska panhandle coalesced into a large supercell in southern Garden county and tracked into Deuel county.  Large hail and funnel clouds were reported.  A separate thunderstorms moved across Perkins county, in southwest Nebraska, and produced large hail.

Hot and very dry conditions persisted into early September across eastern Nebraska, as the first few days of the month saw little or no rainfall along with high temperatures frequently in the 90s.  This pushed the drought intensity into the Exceptional range in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender.  Temperatures moderated somewhat during the middle and end of September.  A mid-month rain event dropped around 1 inch to as much as 3 inches of rain near and southeast of Interstate 80, but amounts in northeast Nebraska were generally a half inch or less. That rain event was generally it for significant rainfall and rain totals for the month as a whole were around 2 inches below normal in northeast Nebraska and an inch or inch and half below normal elsewhere.  Thus the drought intensity remained in the exceptional category in northeast Nebraska and in the Extreme category over much of the rest of eastern Nebraska through September.  The exception was the far southeast corner of the state where the drought intensity moderated slightly into the severe category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought, which began in June, continued to intensify through September as another month of below normal precipitation was observed across western and north central Nebraska. A few significant storm systems did move across the central plains during the month, but produced very little rainfall across the area.  The drought caused a large fluctuations in temperatures with high temperatures being above normal, while low temperatures dropped to below normal values much of the month.  By the end of September, the Drought Monitor had expanded the area under Exceptional Drought (D4) across all of western and north central Nebraska as most places were experiencing a precipitation deficit in excess of eight inches for the year.  Affects of the drought included below normal to near record low flows on many of the rivers and streams across the area, damaged crops and pastures, lowered ground water levels leading to shallow wells drying up, as well as extreme fire danger.  In addition, the drought increased irrigation needs, which led to higher flows along the North Platte River which quickly depleted the water storage at Lake McConaughy.  The abundant dry air caused thunderstorms to produce little or no rainfall, which increased the threat for lightning strike fires.  Many grass fires were observed across the area, and while most were small, several larger fires occurred, requiring higher amounts of resources.  The drought continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued to intensify through September as below normal precipitation was observed across Garden County.  For the month, rainfall was up to 2 inches below normal, while the yearly precipitation deficit was in excess of 8 inches for most locations.  By the end of September, all of Garden County had conditions worsen to Exceptional (D4) Drought. The drought continued into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted into and through October across eastern Nebraska.  Rainfall for the month was near normal over most of the region, the exception was northeast Nebraska where some locations received amounts that were 1.5 inches or so below normal.  Thus, Exceptional drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender.  The drought intensity remained Extreme southeast of there from around Lincoln through Omaha, but improved slightly into the Severe category southeast of Lincoln and Omaha.  Extreme or Exceptional drought conditions persisted over much of the area into November.

For several hours on this Wednesday evening and early Thursday morning, a complex of strong to locally severe thunderstorms rolled from west-to-east across much of South Central Nebraska. Ground-truth severe weather reports were quite limited, with the most noteworthy event occurring around 1030 PM CDT near Hazard in Sherman County where an old barn was destroyed by estimated 70 MPH winds and other nearby outbuilding were damaged. Other estimated or measured wind gusts included 50-55 MPH in Cozad and 53 MPH at the Lexington airport. The first storms to affect the area, including the one that caused the Sherman County damage, spread into several western and northern counties out of the Sandhills region between 930 and 1130 PM and primarily consisted of multicell clusters and line segments. Generally between 1130 PM and 230 AM, a small mesoscale convective system (MCS) organized just north of the Nebraska-Kansas border and rolled east through mainly Harlan, Franklin, Webster, Nuckolls and Thayer Counties. However, these storms yielded no reports of severe weather. On a positive note, this event resulted in the most widespread rainfall in several weeks across the drought-stricken area, with most of the 24 counties measuring between 0.25 and 1.50 inches, along with some locally higher amounts including 2.04 near Hildreth in Franklin County. On the large-scale aloft, this round of storms developed within modest quasi-zonal flow between the southern periphery of a shortwave trough crossing the Northern Plains states and a mid-upper ridge centered over northern Texas. At the surface, the initial afternoon and early evening storms that formed in western and north central Nebraska developed near a slow-moving cold front and associated pre-frontal trough axis. With surface dewpoints averaging well into the upper 60s-low 70s F in South Central Nebraska, a rather unstable airmass was in place and mesoscale parameters around the time of the Sherman County wind damage featured at least 2000-3000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE and 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A three-story barn was destroyed, along with other nearby outbuildings. The time of damage is estimated from radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although August rainfall deficits across the majority of the 24-county area were not as extreme as those in July, below normal rain was still the main story, plunging South Central Nebraska even deeper into the worst drought in several decades. According to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor throughout the month, category D3 extreme drought remained in place across most of the area and expanded farther east into all or part of York, Clay, Fillmore, Nuckolls and Thayer Counties. However, the worst conditions as indicated by category D4 exceptional drought continued to focus north of Interstate 80, gripping much of Valley, Greeley, Sherman and Howard Counties along with small slivers of Nance and Dawson Counties. The only limited part of the area to hold onto category D2 severe drought throughout the month included southern portions of Nuckolls and Thayer Counties. Focusing on August rainfall, the majority of South Central Nebraska tallied between 1.00 and 2.25 inches, or generally between 25-60 percent of normal. On the drier side of things, a few of the lowest monthly amounts per NWS cooperative observers including 0.27 near Orleans, 0.44 at Cambridge and 0.52 near St. Paul. The monthly total of 0.94 at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island marked the 10th-driest August on record out of 117 years. In contrast, a few of the most fortunate sites included Cozad (3.54), Osceola (2.71), Holdrege (2.44) and Hastings airport (2.39). Over the longer-term period spanning June 1st-August 15th, rainfall in South Central Nebraska averaged only 10-30 percent of normal for most places north of Interstate 80, and 40-70 percent of normal for most areas between I-80 and the Kansas border. Area rivers continued to reflect significant drought impacts in August as well, including the Platte which remained essentially dry from near Grand Island downstream to Columbus. Following a notably hot June and especially July, there was some good news temperature-wise during August as the mean monthly temperature across most of the area checked in within 1 degree of normal. The drought situation by late-summer reflected a sharp downward spiral since early June when the entire area was still free of all drought categories. Looking back a few months, although some areas near the Kansas border and also west of Highway 281 started to dry out quite a bit in May, drought conditions really took hold during June and especially July as total rainfall for these two months registered only 10-50 percent of normal across most of the area. Furthermore, average maximum temperatures for the combined months of June and July registered at least 6-8 degrees above normal, essentially baking moisture-starved maturing crops. As of August 8th, the USDA had designated 22 of the 24 counties as primary counties in a federal drought disaster area. For the week ending August 12th, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that crop conditions continued to decline, with many corn fields being chopped for silage or baled for hay. State-averaged topsoil and subsoil moisture continued to run 96 percent short to very short. Poor to very poor crop conditions were reported as 41 percent for corn, 46 percent for soybeans and 68 percent for alfalfa. Pasture and range conditions rated 92 percent poor to very poor.

Poor to very poor crop conditions were reported as 41 percent for corn, 46 percent for soybeans and 68 percent for alfalfa. Pasture and range conditions rated 92 percent poor to very poor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought, which began in June, continued to intensify through September as another month of below normal precipitation was observed across western and north central Nebraska. A few significant storm systems did move across the central plains during the month, but produced very little rainfall across the area.  The drought caused a large fluctuations in temperatures with high temperatures being above normal, while low temperatures dropped to below normal values much of the month.  By the end of September, the Drought Monitor had expanded the area under Exceptional Drought (D4) across all of western and north central Nebraska as most places were experiencing a precipitation deficit in excess of eight inches for the year.  Affects of the drought included below normal to near record low flows on many of the rivers and streams across the area, damaged crops and pastures, lowered ground water levels leading to shallow wells drying up, as well as extreme fire danger.  In addition, the drought increased irrigation needs, which led to higher flows along the North Platte River which quickly depleted the water storage at Lake McConaughy.  The abundant dry air caused thunderstorms to produce little or no rainfall, which increased the threat for lightning strike fires.  Many grass fires were observed across the area, and while most were small, several larger fires occurred, requiring higher amounts of resources.  The drought continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued to intensify through September as below normal precipitation was observed across the western two-thirds of Cherry County. For the month, rainfall in these areas was 1 to 2 inches below normal, while the yearly precipitation deficit was in excess of 8 inches. By the end of September,  conditions worsened from Extreme (D3) to Exceptional (D4) drought and continued into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted into and through October across eastern Nebraska.  Rainfall for the month was near normal over most of the region, the exception was northeast Nebraska where some locations received amounts that were 1.5 inches or so below normal.  Thus, Exceptional drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender.  The drought intensity remained Extreme southeast of there from around Lincoln through Omaha, but improved slightly into the Severe category southeast of Lincoln and Omaha.  Extreme or Exceptional drought conditions persisted over much of the area into November.

Between 430 PM and 730 PM CDT on this Tuesday, a slow-moving complex of severe thunderstorms with large hail pounded a localized area north of state Highway 92, primarily affecting western Nance, southeastern Greeley and northern Howard Counties. Despite this fairly limited areal coverage, there were numerous reports of hail ranging from nickel up to golf ball size, with most of these reports concentrated along a roughly 25-mile long corridor extending from west of Belgrade southwest through the Wolbach and Elba areas. Golf ball size hail reportedly shattered a windshield three miles northwest of Wolbach. Despite the slow-moving nature of these storms, the highest-known rainfall total within the affected area was only 1.34 inches in Wolbach. The initial severe storm rapidly intensified across western Nance County between 430 and 5 PM CDT before drifting slowly west-southwest toward Wolbach and Elba during the ensuing few hours. At times, the dominant storm core took on supercell characteristics as it merged with other storms developing in Greeley County. Finally between 7 and 730 PM CDT, the storm complex weakened over Howard County and soon dissipated, with no further thunderstorm development noted within South Central Nebraska. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was driven by transient shortwave energy passing through northwest flow along the eastern periphery of an expansive mid-upper ridge centered over the Four Corners and Intermountain West regions. Features at the surface were fairly subtle, with storm initiation likely sparked by mesoscale convergence along a weak trough axis. Mesoscale severe weather parameters at mid-afternoon included around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE but no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters started falling around 520 pm CDT and continued to fall until about 550 pm CDT. The ground was completely covered with hail and visibility was poor as the hail fell.

By the end of September, Garfield County had conditions worsen from Extreme (D3) to Exceptional (D4) Drought. The drought continued into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought, which began in June, continued to intensify through September as another month of below normal precipitation was observed across western and north central Nebraska. A few significant storm systems did move across the central plains during the month, but produced very little rainfall across the area.  The drought caused a large fluctuations in temperatures with high temperatures being above normal, while low temperatures dropped to below normal values much of the month.  By the end of September, the Drought Monitor had expanded the area under Exceptional Drought (D4) across all of western and north central Nebraska as most places were experiencing a precipitation deficit in excess of eight inches for the year.  Affects of the drought included below normal to near record low flows on many of the rivers and streams across the area, damaged crops and pastures, lowered ground water levels leading to shallow wells drying up, as well as extreme fire danger.  In addition, the drought increased irrigation needs, which led to higher flows along the North Platte River which quickly depleted the water storage at Lake McConaughy.  The abundant dry air caused thunderstorms to produce little or no rainfall, which increased the threat for lightning strike fires.  Many grass fires were observed across the area, and while most were small, several larger fires occurred, requiring higher amounts of resources.  The drought continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued to intensify through September as below normal precipitation was observed across Keith County. For the month, rainfall was up to 2 inches below normal, while the yearly precipitation deficit was in excess of 8 inches for most locations. Keith County remained in Exceptional (D4) Drought going into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level system and deep surface low pressure tracked across the region October 17th and 18th.  Very strong northwest winds of 35 to 50 mph with gusts exceeding 65 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearby Thedford AWOS recorded sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph with gusts to around 60 mph from mid morning through late afternoon.  Winds of this magnitude were estimated across Hooker County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thursday evening high based thunderstorms produced lightning that ignited four wildfires in Keith County and one in Arthur County. The wildfires burned about 60,000 acres of mainly grasslands, but also threatened dozens of summer houses, town houses, condominiums and rental cabins near the Bayside Golf Club on the southwest shore of Lake McConaughy during the Eagle Canyon Hideaway Fire. One wildfire in northeast Keith County spread into southwest McPherson County. The wildfires were contained overnight where loss of grasslands for grazing could exceed 500 thousand dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning strikes ignited four wildfires in Keith County that burned grassland and timbers overnight and were contained on the 3rd by noon time. The wildfires occurred at the Eagle Canyon Hideaway that burned up to 3,000 acres of grassland and timber, at the Otter Creek area that burned up to 40,000 acres, at the Ackley Valley on the Hawthorn Ranch that burned up to 6,000, and at the Glen Echo area that burned an estimated 7,500 acres. The Glen Echo wildfire spread into southwest McPherson County before containment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 430 PM and 730 PM CDT on this Tuesday, a slow-moving complex of severe thunderstorms with large hail pounded a localized area north of state Highway 92, primarily affecting western Nance, southeastern Greeley and northern Howard Counties. Despite this fairly limited areal coverage, there were numerous reports of hail ranging from nickel up to golf ball size, with most of these reports concentrated along a roughly 25-mile long corridor extending from west of Belgrade southwest through the Wolbach and Elba areas. Golf ball size hail reportedly shattered a windshield three miles northwest of Wolbach. Despite the slow-moving nature of these storms, the highest-known rainfall total within the affected area was only 1.34 inches in Wolbach. The initial severe storm rapidly intensified across western Nance County between 430 and 5 PM CDT before drifting slowly west-southwest toward Wolbach and Elba during the ensuing few hours. At times, the dominant storm core took on supercell characteristics as it merged with other storms developing in Greeley County. Finally between 7 and 730 PM CDT, the storm complex weakened over Howard County and soon dissipated, with no further thunderstorm development noted within South Central Nebraska. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was driven by transient shortwave energy passing through northwest flow along the eastern periphery of an expansive mid-upper ridge centered over the Four Corners and Intermountain West regions. Features at the surface were fairly subtle, with storm initiation likely sparked by mesoscale convergence along a weak trough axis. Mesoscale severe weather parameters at mid-afternoon included around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE but no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear.
An intense upper level system and deep surface low pressure tracked across the region October 17th and 18th.  Very strong northwest winds of 35 to 50 mph with gusts exceeding 65 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearby North Platte ASOS and Thedford AWOS each recorded sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts to around 60 mph from mid morning through late afternoon.  Winds of this magnitude were estimated across Logan County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearby North Platte ASOS and Thedford AWOS each recorded sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts to around 60 mph from mid morning through late afternoon.  Winds of this magnitude were estimated across McPherson County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued through October as below normal precipitation was observed. For the month, rainfall was near 1 inch below normal, while the yearly precipitation deficit was in excess of 9 inches. The exceptional (D4) drought continued into November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure over Minnesota and Wisconsin brought a prolonged period of  very strong winds to much of eastern Nebraska.  Especially hard hit was an area from around Omaha west through Columbus and then north through Albion and Norfolk.  Wind gusts of 58 to 63 mph were measured across much of that area, with a prolonged period of sustained winds of 35 to 43 mph observed from around Columbus north and northwest through Albion and Norfolk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 35 to 43 mph and with a peak gust of 63 mph was recorded by the Awos at Columbus.  The winds caused scattered tree and sign damage around Columbus and also flipped several empty boat lifts at area lakes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Platte ASOS recorded sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts to around 60 mph from mid morning through late afternoon.  Winds of this magnitude occurred across Lincoln County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level system and deep surface low pressure tracked across the region October 17th and 18th.  Very strong northwest winds of 35 to 50 mph with gusts exceeding 65 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broken Bow ASOS recorded frequent sustained winds around 50 mph with gusts of 65 to 70 mph from mid morning through mid afternoon.  Winds of this magnitude occurred across Custer County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted into and through October across eastern Nebraska.  Rainfall for the month was near normal over most of the region, the exception was northeast Nebraska where some locations received amounts that were 1.5 inches or so below normal.  Thus, Exceptional drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender.  The drought intensity remained Extreme southeast of there from around Lincoln through Omaha, but improved slightly into the Severe category southeast of Lincoln and Omaha.  Extreme or Exceptional drought conditions persisted over much of the area into November.

An intense low pressure system moved across the Central Plains states producing wind gusts of 50 to 65 mph from mid-morning through much of the afternoon.  Blowing dirt reduced visibility locally to less than 1/4 mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts are estimated to have reached 60 mph based on reports from mesonet sites in surrounding counties.

Primarily between midnight-5 AM CDT on this early Wednesday morning, a persistent round of thunderstorms streamed across much of Dawson County, but especially focused a few miles either side of the Interstate 80 corridor. Although none of these storms reached severe-criteria, hail up to nickel size was reported in Cozad, along with multiple waves of heavy rainfall. By the time storms moved out of Dawson County before sunrise, most of the I-80 corridor through the county had measured at least 1-2 inches of rain, with the NWS cooperative observer in Cozad leading the way with 3.07. For Cozad, this event featured more rain than the past two months combined, and briefly helped ease the expanding extreme drought situation within the area. Although Dawson County experienced the strongest, most concentrated zone of thunderstorms within South Central Nebraska, most areas southwest of a line from Loup City to Hebron also experienced storm activity. This early morning episode of elevated multicell convection was largely tied to a zone of mid level moisture convergence and thrived despite limited large scale forcing with Nebraska positioned along the northern periphery of an expansive mid-upper ridge centered over the Southern Plains. Around the time of the nickel size hail in Cozad, the mesoscale environment featured around 1000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE but only around 25 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail occurred for five minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall intensified during the morning over Dundy county then slowly declined through the afternoon.  A narrow band of 6+ inches of snowfall fell from the northcentral part of the county southeast to near Benklemen.  The highest snowfall of over 8 inches was reported 5 NW of Benklemen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall started around 400 MST,  meeting local heavy snow criteria around 1100 MST and lasting until 1600 MST.  A total of 8 to 9 inches of snow was measured 5 NW of Benkelman by 1400 MST.  At 19 N of Parks 7 inches of snow was measured.  At 13 NNE of Parks between 5 and 6 inches of snow was estimated to have fallen.
