Despite this fairly limited areal coverage, there were numerous reports of hail ranging from nickel up to golf ball size, with most of these reports concentrated along a roughly 25-mile long corridor extending from west of Belgrade southwest through the Wolbach and Elba areas. Golf ball size hail reportedly shattered a windshield three miles northwest of Wolbach. Despite the slow-moving nature of these storms, the highest-known rainfall total within the affected area was only 1.34 inches in Wolbach. The initial severe storm rapidly intensified across western Nance County between 430 and 5 PM CDT before drifting slowly west-southwest toward Wolbach and Elba during the ensuing few hours. At times, the dominant storm core took on supercell characteristics as it merged with other storms developing in Greeley County. Finally between 7 and 730 PM CDT, the storm complex weakened over Howard County and soon dissipated, with no further thunderstorm development noted within South Central Nebraska. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was driven by transient shortwave energy passing through northwest flow along the eastern periphery of an expansive mid-upper ridge centered over the Four Corners and Intermountain West regions. Features at the surface were fairly subtle, with storm initiation likely sparked by mesoscale convergence along a weak trough axis. Mesoscale severe weather parameters at mid-afternoon included around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE but no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear.

A wildfire fanned by high winds caused a fire to quickly spread southeast, crossing Interstate 80 and then burning for an additional 12 miles southeast into northeast Perkins County. The wildfire reached up to 1.5 miles wide. The wildfire destroyed two occupied homes, one abandoned home and various outbuildings were destroyed as well. in addition to building structures the wildfire destroyed several pieces of farm implements, while several power poles and culverts were damaged. The area burned was estimated at 10,000 acres of farm flat and rolling hills.  Three firemen developed eye problems with one treated for an unknown illness. The wildfire led to evacuation orders for those in the path of the wildfire from east of Paxton to the south edge of the Keith-Perkins County Line in the southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire started about one mile north of Interstate 80 near Paxton that was fanned by high winds. The high winds caused the wildfire to quickly spread southeast, crossing Interstate 80 and then burning for an additional 12 miles southeast into northeast Perkins County. The wildfire reached up to 1.5 miles wide. The wildfire destroyed two occupied homes, one abandoned home and various outbuildings were destroyed as well. In addition to building structures the wildfire destroyed several pieces of farm implements, while several power poles and culverts were damaged. The area burned was estimated at 10,000 acres of farm flat and rolling hills. Three firemen developed eye problems with one treated for an unknown illness. The wildfire led to evacuation orders for those in the path of the wildfire from east of Paxton to the south edge of the Keith-Perkins County Line in the southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough of low pressure moved across Nebraska on the afternoon of September 3rd.  Scattered thunderstorms developed along the trough, two of which produced severe hail and winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The damaging winds blew down several tree limbs. The size of the limbs were unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed along a cold front on the afternoon of September 4th.  One storm briefly became severe and produced wind gusts up to 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were additional public reports of 45 to 55 m.p.h. wind gusts in the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought, which began in June, continued to intensify through September as another month of below normal precipitation was observed across western and north central Nebraska. A few significant storm systems did move across the central plains during the month, but produced very little rainfall across the area.  The drought caused a large fluctuations in temperatures with high temperatures being above normal, while low temperatures dropped to below normal values much of the month.  By the end of September, the Drought Monitor had expanded the area under Exceptional Drought (D4) across all of western and north central Nebraska as most places were experiencing a precipitation deficit in excess of eight inches for the year.  Affects of the drought included below normal to near record low flows on many of the rivers and streams across the area, damaged crops and pastures, lowered ground water levels leading to shallow wells drying up, as well as extreme fire danger.  In addition, the drought increased irrigation needs, which led to higher flows along the North Platte River which quickly depleted the water storage at Lake McConaughy.  The abundant dry air caused thunderstorms to produce little or no rainfall, which increased the threat for lightning strike fires.  Many grass fires were observed across the area, and while most were small, several larger fires occurred, requiring higher amounts of resources.  The drought continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued to intensify through September as below normal precipitation was observed across Keya Paha County. For the month, rainfall was up to 2 inches below normal, while the yearly precipitation deficit was in excess of 8 inches for most locations. Keya Paha County began the month with Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) drought, while the entire county worsened to Exceptional (D4) drought going into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted into and through October across eastern Nebraska.  Rainfall for the month was near normal over most of the region, the exception was northeast Nebraska where some locations received amounts that were 1.5 inches or so below normal.  Thus, Exceptional drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender.  The drought intensity remained Extreme southeast of there from around Lincoln through Omaha, but improved slightly into the Severe category southeast of Lincoln and Omaha.  Extreme or Exceptional drought conditions persisted over much of the area into November.

Although August rainfall deficits across the majority of the 24-county area were not as extreme as those in July, below normal rain was still the main story, plunging South Central Nebraska even deeper into the worst drought in several decades. According to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor throughout the month, category D3 extreme drought remained in place across most of the area and expanded farther east into all or part of York, Clay, Fillmore, Nuckolls and Thayer Counties. However, the worst conditions as indicated by category D4 exceptional drought continued to focus north of Interstate 80, gripping much of Valley, Greeley, Sherman and Howard Counties along with small slivers of Nance and Dawson Counties. The only limited part of the area to hold onto category D2 severe drought throughout the month included southern portions of Nuckolls and Thayer Counties. Focusing on August rainfall, the majority of South Central Nebraska tallied between 1.00 and 2.25 inches, or generally between 25-60 percent of normal. On the drier side of things, a few of the lowest monthly amounts per NWS cooperative observers including 0.27 near Orleans, 0.44 at Cambridge and 0.52 near St. Paul. The monthly total of 0.94 at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island marked the 10th-driest August on record out of 117 years. In contrast, a few of the most fortunate sites included Cozad (3.54), Osceola (2.71), Holdrege (2.44) and Hastings airport (2.39). Over the longer-term period spanning June 1st-August 15th, rainfall in South Central Nebraska averaged only 10-30 percent of normal for most places north of Interstate 80, and 40-70 percent of normal for most areas between I-80 and the Kansas border. Area rivers continued to reflect significant drought impacts in August as well, including the Platte which remained essentially dry from near Grand Island downstream to Columbus. Following a notably hot June and especially July, there was some good news temperature-wise during August as the mean monthly temperature across most of the area checked in within 1 degree of normal. The drought situation by late-summer reflected a sharp downward spiral since early June when the entire area was still free of all drought categories. Looking back a few months, although some areas near the Kansas border and also west of Highway 281 started to dry out quite a bit in May, drought conditions really took hold during June and especially July as total rainfall for these two months registered only 10-50 percent of normal across most of the area. Furthermore, average maximum temperatures for the combined months of June and July registered at least 6-8 degrees above normal, essentially baking moisture-starved maturing crops. As of August 8th, the USDA had designated 22 of the 24 counties as primary counties in a federal drought disaster area. For the week ending August 12th, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that crop conditions continued to decline, with many corn fields being chopped for silage or baled for hay. State-averaged topsoil and subsoil moisture continued to run 96 percent short to very short. Poor to very poor crop conditions were reported as 41 percent for corn, 46 percent for soybeans and 68 percent for alfalfa. Pasture and range conditions rated 92 percent poor to very poor.

A deep upper trough moved across the Rockies and into the central plains bringing near-record high temperatures ahead of it and a very cold airmass behind it. Snow fell across much of the High Plains and Dakotas while isolated severe storms developed along a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind and thunder were reported at the Lincoln airport as low level jet winds were mixed down to the surface due to nearby thunderstorms.

Storm total snow accumulations ranged from two to six inches. The combination of falling snow, gusty north winds and low visibilities created hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Hemingford measured four inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted into and through October across eastern Nebraska.  Rainfall for the month was near normal over most of the region, the exception was northeast Nebraska where some locations received amounts that were 1.5 inches or so below normal.  Thus, Exceptional drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender.  The drought intensity remained Extreme southeast of there from around Lincoln through Omaha, but improved slightly into the Severe category southeast of Lincoln and Omaha.  Extreme or Exceptional drought conditions persisted over much of the area into November.

Drought conditions continued over all of the area with below normal rainfall keeping soil conditions dry. Stress on crops continued even though August was less hot than July, with temperatures averaging only a little above normal. Crop damage was quite evident, though the amount of reduced yields and other damage which might become evident at harvest was uncertain. While reported water supply problems were not extreme, many local governments had water use restrictions in place. Drought was generally listed as severe to extreme for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued to aggravate existing long term dry soil conditions, and stress on crops continued despite less hot weather than in July. Crop damage including certain reduced crop yields was very apparent, although the amount of yield reduction and other damage was still highly uncertain before harvest. Corn continued to be the crop suspected of enduring the most damage, with some recovery potential for soybeans in the less hot weather. The drought was also leading to local water use restrictions.

The drought, which began in June, continued to intensify through September as another month of below normal precipitation was observed across western and north central Nebraska. A few significant storm systems did move across the central plains during the month, but produced very little rainfall across the area.  The drought caused a large fluctuations in temperatures with high temperatures being above normal, while low temperatures dropped to below normal values much of the month.  By the end of September, the Drought Monitor had expanded the area under Exceptional Drought (D4) across all of western and north central Nebraska as most places were experiencing a precipitation deficit in excess of eight inches for the year.  Affects of the drought included below normal to near record low flows on many of the rivers and streams across the area, damaged crops and pastures, lowered ground water levels leading to shallow wells drying up, as well as extreme fire danger.  In addition, the drought increased irrigation needs, which led to higher flows along the North Platte River which quickly depleted the water storage at Lake McConaughy.  The abundant dry air caused thunderstorms to produce little or no rainfall, which increased the threat for lightning strike fires.  Many grass fires were observed across the area, and while most were small, several larger fires occurred, requiring higher amounts of resources.  The drought continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued to intensify through September as below normal precipitation was observed across Holt County.  For the month, rainfall was 1 to 3 inches below normal, while the yearly precipitation deficit was in excess of 8 inches for most locations.  By the end of September, Holt County had conditions worsen from Extreme (D3) to Exceptional (D4) Drought. The drought continued into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning produced by high based thunderstorms ignited the Wellnitz Fire on August 29th. The fire burned over 77,159 acres of land, which included 44,159 acres in Nebraska.  The fire started 10 miles north of Hay Springs, then spread into South Dakota onto the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation. The fire burned timber and grass, along with destroying up to 60 outbuildings, two homes, two commercial properties, and 100 power poles across Sheridan County.  An  additional 10 homes were damaged. The wildfire continued into September and was reported 100 percent contained on September 4th. The Nebraska National Guard was deployed (14 personnel) to help fight the fire, which included the use of two type three helicopters and one Blackhawk helicopter. Many roads in the area were closed as the fire burned. Cooperating agencies across Nebraska deployed 350 personnel, while 45 volunteer fire departments from across Nebraska and South Dakota responded to the fire-fighting efforts.  Three non life-threatening injuries were reported from the fire and fire-fighting efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire ignited by lightning in late August continued into September to burn approximately 44,159 acres in Nebraska before being contained on September 4th. The wildfire spread north into South Dakota for a total of 77,159 acres burned before containment. Initial assessments showed the wildfire burned approximately 100 power poles. The Rushville Sheriff requested an Evacuate Immediate (NWEM) message as the fire spread into parks and to alert homeowners and campers in the area. The fire stats showed 50 to 60 outbuildings destroyed, and approximately 10 homes damaged as well as two homes and two commercial properties destroyed.  The Nebraska National Guard was deployed (14 personnel) to help fight the fire, which included the use of two type three helicopters and one Blackhawk helicopter.  Many roads in the area were closed as the fire burned.  Cooperating agencies across Nebraska deployed 350 personnel, while 45 volunteer fire departments from across Nebraska and South Dakota responded to the fire-fighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system and associated surface cold front continued to slowly move east across the area producing strong and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kings Canyon RAWS measured sustained winds in excess of 40 mph with a peak gust of 64 mph at 1022 MST.

Lightning produced by high based thunderstorms ignited the Wellnitz Fire. The fire burned 77,159 acres of land, starting near 10 miles north of Hay Springs that spread to across the South Dakota border onto the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation. The fire burned timber and grass, along with destroying up to 60 outbuildings and damage to 10 homes. The wildfire continued into September and was reported 100 percent contained on September 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire was ignited by lightning August 29th that spread quickly north across grassland and timbers across northwest Sheridan County. The wildfire burned approximate 44,159 acres in Nebraska and moved north into South Dakota. Initial assessments showed the wildfire burned approximately 100 power poles. The Rushville Sheriff requested an Evacuate Immediate (NWEM) message as the fire spread into parks and to alert homeowners and campers in the area. The fire stats showed 50 to 60 outbuildings destroyed, and approximately 10 homes damaged. The wildfire continued into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning produced by high based thunderstorms moving northeast across Sheridan County ignited a wildfire August 29th that burned a total of 77,159 acres of land. The wildfire spread across the South Dakota border into the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation. The wildfire continued into September and was contained on September 4th. The Wellnitz fire is referenced as part of the Region 23 Complex Fire by FEMA Region VII.

The Rushville Sheriff requested an Evacuate Immediate (NWEM) message as the fire spread into parks and to alert homeowners and campers in the area. The fire stats showed 50 to 60 outbuildings destroyed, and approximately 10 homes damaged. The wildfire continued into September.

Although August rainfall deficits across the majority of the 24-county area were not as extreme as those in July, below normal rain was still the main story, plunging South Central Nebraska even deeper into the worst drought in several decades. According to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor throughout the month, category D3 extreme drought remained in place across most of the area and expanded farther east into all or part of York, Clay, Fillmore, Nuckolls and Thayer Counties. However, the worst conditions as indicated by category D4 exceptional drought continued to focus north of Interstate 80, gripping much of Valley, Greeley, Sherman and Howard Counties along with small slivers of Nance and Dawson Counties. The only limited part of the area to hold onto category D2 severe drought throughout the month included southern portions of Nuckolls and Thayer Counties. Focusing on August rainfall, the majority of South Central Nebraska tallied between 1.00 and 2.25 inches, or generally between 25-60 percent of normal. On the drier side of things, a few of the lowest monthly amounts per NWS cooperative observers including 0.27 near Orleans, 0.44 at Cambridge and 0.52 near St. Paul. The monthly total of 0.94 at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island marked the 10th-driest August on record out of 117 years. In contrast, a few of the most fortunate sites included Cozad (3.54), Osceola (2.71), Holdrege (2.44) and Hastings airport (2.39). Over the longer-term period spanning June 1st-August 15th, rainfall in South Central Nebraska averaged only 10-30 percent of normal for most places north of Interstate 80, and 40-70 percent of normal for most areas between I-80 and the Kansas border. Area rivers continued to reflect significant drought impacts in August as well, including the Platte which remained essentially dry from near Grand Island downstream to Columbus. Following a notably hot June and especially July, there was some good news temperature-wise during August as the mean monthly temperature across most of the area checked in within 1 degree of normal. The drought situation by late-summer reflected a sharp downward spiral since early June when the entire area was still free of all drought categories. Looking back a few months, although some areas near the Kansas border and also west of Highway 281 started to dry out quite a bit in May, drought conditions really took hold during June and especially July as total rainfall for these two months registered only 10-50 percent of normal across most of the area. Furthermore, average maximum temperatures for the combined months of June and July registered at least 6-8 degrees above normal, essentially baking moisture-starved maturing crops. As of August 8th, the USDA had designated 22 of the 24 counties as primary counties in a federal drought disaster area. For the week ending August 12th, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that crop conditions continued to decline, with many corn fields being chopped for silage or baled for hay. State-averaged topsoil and subsoil moisture continued to run 96 percent short to very short. Poor to very poor crop conditions were reported as 41 percent for corn, 46 percent for soybeans and 68 percent for alfalfa. Pasture and range conditions rated 92 percent poor to very poor.

Starting on Wednesday the 17th and then peaking on Thursday the 18th, South Central Nebraska experienced its overall windiest two-day period in several years. Blowing out of the northwest on both days, at least minor visibility restrictions due to blowing dust were common, and a few wildfires were sparked including a rather large one on the 17th in western Fillmore County. ||Focusing on the 18th, this was the windiest of the two days, and featured several hours of sustained speeds generally 35-45 MPH and frequent gusts of at least 50-60 MPH. The highest measured wind gust of the day at automated airport sites, 70 MPH, occurred at Evelyn Sharp Field in Ord, with a few other peak gusts at airports including 63 MPH at Grand Island, 60 MPH at Lexington and 59 MPH at Hastings, Aurora and York. While at least minor damage was likely widespread across the area, known damage reports included a trailer home with half its roof torn off in Cozad and a large tree branch that fell onto a moving car in Hastings. These intense winds were driven by deep mixing within an impressive surface pressure gradient between a 988 millibar low in northern Minnesota and a 1029 millibar high centered over Idaho. Attesting to the strength of this surface pressure gradient, late morning observations revealed a 20-millibar gradient across the state of Nebraska alone, ranging from 1016mb in the far southwest corner to 996mb in the far northeast.  This high wind event got underway unusually early in the day for northern parts of the area, as the Ord airport already gusted to 58 MPH prior to 7 AM CDT. Although sustained speeds largely eased below 40 MPH by 5 PM CDT, it remained quite windy well into the night with sustained speeds commonly 30+ MPH and gusts still around 40 MPH or higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearby AWOS recorded sustained wind speeds near and in excess of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained over much of eastern Nebraska into November as precipitation continued below normal, generally a half an inch or less (although a little over 1 inch fell over parts of far southeast Nebraska).  These amounts were around an inch below normal.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice through Omaha.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  Extreme or Exceptional drought conditions persisted over much of the area into December.

September marked the fifth consecutive month of well-below normal rainfall across most of the 24-county area, further worsening an already-serious drought situation as the summer and fall of 2012 continued marking its place in history as one of the worst droughts in several decades. Throughout the month, all 24 counties remained under a federally-designated drought disaster. According to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor, the coverage of category D4 exceptional drought expanded early in the month to encompass several more counties and remained firmly in place thereafter. By month's end, exceptional drought was assigned to all or part of 15 of the 24 counties, primarily those north of Interstate 80 and also west of Highway 183. Meanwhile, category D3 extreme drought persisted across most of the other nine counties, while only a small zone of category D2 severe drought held on in portions of Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Taking a closer look at monthly precipitation totals, the vast majority of the area only measured between 0.25-0.70 inches, well under monthly normals generally between 1.75-2.50. Put another way, most of South Central Nebraska received just 10-40 percent of normal September rainfall. Prolonging a trend that started emerging over the past few months, the overall driest conditions focused across the northwest half of the area. Per NWS cooperative observers and a handful of automated stations, a few of the paltriest monthly totals were highlighted by 0.12 at Ord, 0.25 at Arcadia and Gothenburg and 0.30 at Cambridge and Miller. At Ord, this marked the 2nd-driest September on record. A few of the highest monthly rainfall totals across the area, although still well below normal, included 1.75 at Hubbell, 0.87 at Franklin and south of Shickley and 0.84 at Hebron. ||Over the longer-range five month period from May through September, corresponding to the majority of the growing season, most of South Central Nebraska only realized between 20-60 percent of normal rainfall. Far northwest counties were the driest of the dry over this time span, highlighted by Ord with 3.66, merely 22% of the normal of 16.50. Area rivers continued to reflect significant drought impacts in September, with most waterways in the Platte, Loup, Republican and Little Blue basins continuing to flow at stages below to much below normal. For the week ending September 23rd, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that state-averaged subsoil and topsoil moisture rated 98 percent and 95 percent short to very short, respectively. Poor to very poor crop conditions across the state were assigned to 41 percent of the corn crop, 47 percent of soybeans and 59 percent of sorghum. Pasture and range conditions rated 98 percent poor to very poor, a far cry from the 71 percent good to excellent rating of one year ago.

The drought, which began in June, continued to intensify through September as another month of below normal precipitation was observed across western and north central Nebraska. A few significant storm systems did move across the central plains during the month, but produced very little rainfall across the area.  The drought caused a large fluctuations in temperatures with high temperatures being above normal, while low temperatures dropped to below normal values much of the month.  By the end of September, the Drought Monitor had expanded the area under Exceptional Drought (D4) across all of western and north central Nebraska as most places were experiencing a precipitation deficit in excess of eight inches for the year.  Affects of the drought included below normal to near record low flows on many of the rivers and streams across the area, damaged crops and pastures, lowered ground water levels leading to shallow wells drying up, as well as extreme fire danger.  In addition, the drought increased irrigation needs, which led to higher flows along the North Platte River which quickly depleted the water storage at Lake McConaughy.  The abundant dry air caused thunderstorms to produce little or no rainfall, which increased the threat for lightning strike fires.  Many grass fires were observed across the area, and while most were small, several larger fires occurred, requiring higher amounts of resources.  The drought continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued to intensify through September as below normal precipitation was observed across Logan County. For the month, rainfall was 1 to 2 inches below normal, while the yearly precipitation deficit was in excess of 8 inches for most locations. Logan County remained in Exceptional (D4) Drought going into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained over much of eastern Nebraska into November as precipitation continued below normal, generally a half an inch or less (although a little over 1 inch fell over parts of far southeast Nebraska).  These amounts were around an inch below normal.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice through Omaha.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  Extreme or Exceptional drought conditions persisted over much of the area into December.

September marked the fifth consecutive month of well-below normal rainfall across most of the 24-county area, further worsening an already-serious drought situation as the summer and fall of 2012 continued marking its place in history as one of the worst droughts in several decades. Throughout the month, all 24 counties remained under a federally-designated drought disaster. According to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor, the coverage of category D4 exceptional drought expanded early in the month to encompass several more counties and remained firmly in place thereafter. By month's end, exceptional drought was assigned to all or part of 15 of the 24 counties, primarily those north of Interstate 80 and also west of Highway 183. Meanwhile, category D3 extreme drought persisted across most of the other nine counties, while only a small zone of category D2 severe drought held on in portions of Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Taking a closer look at monthly precipitation totals, the vast majority of the area only measured between 0.25-0.70 inches, well under monthly normals generally between 1.75-2.50. Put another way, most of South Central Nebraska received just 10-40 percent of normal September rainfall. Prolonging a trend that started emerging over the past few months, the overall driest conditions focused across the northwest half of the area. Per NWS cooperative observers and a handful of automated stations, a few of the paltriest monthly totals were highlighted by 0.12 at Ord, 0.25 at Arcadia and Gothenburg and 0.30 at Cambridge and Miller. At Ord, this marked the 2nd-driest September on record. A few of the highest monthly rainfall totals across the area, although still well below normal, included 1.75 at Hubbell, 0.87 at Franklin and south of Shickley and 0.84 at Hebron. ||Over the longer-range five month period from May through September, corresponding to the majority of the growing season, most of South Central Nebraska only realized between 20-60 percent of normal rainfall. Far northwest counties were the driest of the dry over this time span, highlighted by Ord with 3.66, merely 22% of the normal of 16.50. Area rivers continued to reflect significant drought impacts in September, with most waterways in the Platte, Loup, Republican and Little Blue basins continuing to flow at stages below to much below normal. For the week ending September 23rd, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that state-averaged subsoil and topsoil moisture rated 98 percent and 95 percent short to very short, respectively. Poor to very poor crop conditions across the state were assigned to 41 percent of the corn crop, 47 percent of soybeans and 59 percent of sorghum. Pasture and range conditions rated 98 percent poor to very poor, a far cry from the 71 percent good to excellent rating of one year ago.

Taking a closer look at monthly precipitation totals, the vast majority of the area only measured between 0.25-0.70 inches, well under monthly normals generally between 1.75-2.50. Put another way, most of South Central Nebraska received just 10-40 percent of normal September rainfall. Prolonging a trend that started emerging over the past few months, the overall driest conditions focused across the northwest half of the area. Per NWS cooperative observers and a handful of automated stations, a few of the paltriest monthly totals were highlighted by 0.12 at Ord, 0.25 at Arcadia and Gothenburg and 0.30 at Cambridge and Miller. At Ord, this marked the 2nd-driest September on record. A few of the highest monthly rainfall totals across the area, although still well below normal, included 1.75 at Hubbell, 0.87 at Franklin and south of Shickley and 0.84 at Hebron. ||Over the longer-range five month period from May through September, corresponding to the majority of the growing season, most of South Central Nebraska only realized between 20-60 percent of normal rainfall. Far northwest counties were the driest of the dry over this time span, highlighted by Ord with 3.66, merely 22% of the normal of 16.50. Area rivers continued to reflect significant drought impacts in September, with most waterways in the Platte, Loup, Republican and Little Blue basins continuing to flow at stages below to much below normal. For the week ending September 23rd, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that state-averaged subsoil and topsoil moisture rated 98 percent and 95 percent short to very short, respectively. Poor to very poor crop conditions across the state were assigned to 41 percent of the corn crop, 47 percent of soybeans and 59 percent of sorghum. Pasture and range conditions rated 98 percent poor to very poor, a far cry from the 71 percent good to excellent rating of one year ago.

Starting on Wednesday the 17th and then peaking on Thursday the 18th, South Central Nebraska experienced its overall windiest two-day period in several years. Blowing out of the northwest on both days, at least minor visibility restrictions due to blowing dust were common, and a few wildfires were sparked including a rather large one on the 17th in western Fillmore County. ||Focusing on the 18th, this was the windiest of the two days, and featured several hours of sustained speeds generally 35-45 MPH and frequent gusts of at least 50-60 MPH. The highest measured wind gust of the day at automated airport sites, 70 MPH, occurred at Evelyn Sharp Field in Ord, with a few other peak gusts at airports including 63 MPH at Grand Island, 60 MPH at Lexington and 59 MPH at Hastings, Aurora and York. While at least minor damage was likely widespread across the area, known damage reports included a trailer home with half its roof torn off in Cozad and a large tree branch that fell onto a moving car in Hastings. These intense winds were driven by deep mixing within an impressive surface pressure gradient between a 988 millibar low in northern Minnesota and a 1029 millibar high centered over Idaho. Attesting to the strength of this surface pressure gradient, late morning observations revealed a 20-millibar gradient across the state of Nebraska alone, ranging from 1016mb in the far southwest corner to 996mb in the far northeast.  This high wind event got underway unusually early in the day for northern parts of the area, as the Ord airport already gusted to 58 MPH prior to 7 AM CDT. Although sustained speeds largely eased below 40 MPH by 5 PM CDT, it remained quite windy well into the night with sustained speeds commonly 30+ MPH and gusts still around 40 MPH or higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearby AWOS recorded sustained wind speeds near and in excess of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought, which began in June, continued to intensify through September as another month of below normal precipitation was observed across western and north central Nebraska. A few significant storm systems did move across the central plains during the month, but produced very little rainfall across the area.  The drought caused a large fluctuations in temperatures with high temperatures being above normal, while low temperatures dropped to below normal values much of the month.  By the end of September, the Drought Monitor had expanded the area under Exceptional Drought (D4) across all of western and north central Nebraska as most places were experiencing a precipitation deficit in excess of eight inches for the year.  Affects of the drought included below normal to near record low flows on many of the rivers and streams across the area, damaged crops and pastures, lowered ground water levels leading to shallow wells drying up, as well as extreme fire danger.  In addition, the drought increased irrigation needs, which led to higher flows along the North Platte River which quickly depleted the water storage at Lake McConaughy.  The abundant dry air caused thunderstorms to produce little or no rainfall, which increased the threat for lightning strike fires.  Many grass fires were observed across the area, and while most were small, several larger fires occurred, requiring higher amounts of resources.  The drought continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued to intensify through September as below normal precipitation was observed across Boyd County. For the month, rainfall was 1 to 3 inches below normal, while the yearly precipitation deficit was in excess of 8 inches for most locations. By the end of September, all of Boyd County had intensified from Extreme (D3) to Exceptional (D4) Drought. These conditions continued into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained over much of eastern Nebraska into November as precipitation continued below normal, generally a half an inch or less (although a little over 1 inch fell over parts of far southeast Nebraska).  These amounts were around an inch below normal.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice through Omaha.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  Extreme or Exceptional drought conditions persisted over much of the area into December.

Pasture and range conditions rated 98 percent poor to very poor, a far cry from the 71 percent good to excellent rating of one year ago.

September marked the fifth consecutive month of well-below normal rainfall across most of the 24-county area, further worsening an already-serious drought situation as the summer and fall of 2012 continued marking its place in history as one of the worst droughts in several decades. Throughout the month, all 24 counties remained under a federally-designated drought disaster. According to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor, the coverage of category D4 exceptional drought expanded early in the month to encompass several more counties and remained firmly in place thereafter. By month's end, exceptional drought was assigned to all or part of 15 of the 24 counties, primarily those north of Interstate 80 and also west of Highway 183. Meanwhile, category D3 extreme drought persisted across most of the other nine counties, while only a small zone of category D2 severe drought held on in portions of Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Taking a closer look at monthly precipitation totals, the vast majority of the area only measured between 0.25-0.70 inches, well under monthly normals generally between 1.75-2.50. Put another way, most of South Central Nebraska received just 10-40 percent of normal September rainfall. Prolonging a trend that started emerging over the past few months, the overall driest conditions focused across the northwest half of the area. Per NWS cooperative observers and a handful of automated stations, a few of the paltriest monthly totals were highlighted by 0.12 at Ord, 0.25 at Arcadia and Gothenburg and 0.30 at Cambridge and Miller. At Ord, this marked the 2nd-driest September on record. A few of the highest monthly rainfall totals across the area, although still well below normal, included 1.75 at Hubbell, 0.87 at Franklin and south of Shickley and 0.84 at Hebron. ||Over the longer-range five month period from May through September, corresponding to the majority of the growing season, most of South Central Nebraska only realized between 20-60 percent of normal rainfall. Far northwest counties were the driest of the dry over this time span, highlighted by Ord with 3.66, merely 22% of the normal of 16.50. Area rivers continued to reflect significant drought impacts in September, with most waterways in the Platte, Loup, Republican and Little Blue basins continuing to flow at stages below to much below normal. For the week ending September 23rd, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that state-averaged subsoil and topsoil moisture rated 98 percent and 95 percent short to very short, respectively. Poor to very poor crop conditions across the state were assigned to 41 percent of the corn crop, 47 percent of soybeans and 59 percent of sorghum. Pasture and range conditions rated 98 percent poor to very poor, a far cry from the 71 percent good to excellent rating of one year ago.

Starting on Wednesday the 17th and then peaking on Thursday the 18th, South Central Nebraska experienced its overall windiest two-day period in several years. Blowing out of the northwest on both days, at least minor visibility restrictions due to blowing dust were common, and a few wildfires were sparked including a rather large one on the 17th in western Fillmore County. ||Focusing on the 18th, this was the windiest of the two days, and featured several hours of sustained speeds generally 35-45 MPH and frequent gusts of at least 50-60 MPH. The highest measured wind gust of the day at automated airport sites, 70 MPH, occurred at Evelyn Sharp Field in Ord, with a few other peak gusts at airports including 63 MPH at Grand Island, 60 MPH at Lexington and 59 MPH at Hastings, Aurora and York. While at least minor damage was likely widespread across the area, known damage reports included a trailer home with half its roof torn off in Cozad and a large tree branch that fell onto a moving car in Hastings. These intense winds were driven by deep mixing within an impressive surface pressure gradient between a 988 millibar low in northern Minnesota and a 1029 millibar high centered over Idaho. Attesting to the strength of this surface pressure gradient, late morning observations revealed a 20-millibar gradient across the state of Nebraska alone, ranging from 1016mb in the far southwest corner to 996mb in the far northeast.  This high wind event got underway unusually early in the day for northern parts of the area, as the Ord airport already gusted to 58 MPH prior to 7 AM CDT. Although sustained speeds largely eased below 40 MPH by 5 PM CDT, it remained quite windy well into the night with sustained speeds commonly 30+ MPH and gusts still around 40 MPH or higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 mph occurred at the Aurora airport at 955 CST. Also, sustained wind speeds of 40 to 45 mph occurred at least periodically between 935 am CDT and 1515 pm CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures reaching the 90s and high humidity with dew points in the 70s caused excessive heat during the afternoon of August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures warmed into the 90s while dew points were in the 70s, resulting in heat index readings reaching the 100 to 105 degree range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought, which began in June, continued to intensify through September as another month of below normal precipitation was observed across western and north central Nebraska. A few significant storm systems did move across the central plains during the month, but produced very little rainfall across the area.  The drought caused a large fluctuations in temperatures with high temperatures being above normal, while low temperatures dropped to below normal values much of the month.  By the end of September, the Drought Monitor had expanded the area under Exceptional Drought (D4) across all of western and north central Nebraska as most places were experiencing a precipitation deficit in excess of eight inches for the year.  Affects of the drought included below normal to near record low flows on many of the rivers and streams across the area, damaged crops and pastures, lowered ground water levels leading to shallow wells drying up, as well as extreme fire danger.  In addition, the drought increased irrigation needs, which led to higher flows along the North Platte River which quickly depleted the water storage at Lake McConaughy.  The abundant dry air caused thunderstorms to produce little or no rainfall, which increased the threat for lightning strike fires.  Many grass fires were observed across the area, and while most were small, several larger fires occurred, requiring higher amounts of resources.  The drought continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued to intensify through September as below normal precipitation was observed across Loup County. For the month, rainfall was 1 to 2 inches below normal, while the yearly precipitation deficit was in excess of 8 inches for most locations. By the end of September, all of Loup County saw conditions worsen from Extreme (D3) to Exceptional (D4) drought.  These conditions continued into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained over much of eastern Nebraska into November as precipitation continued below normal, generally a half an inch or less (although a little over 1 inch fell over parts of far southeast Nebraska).  These amounts were around an inch below normal.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice through Omaha.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  Extreme or Exceptional drought conditions persisted over much of the area into December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although October was not quite as dry as September across most of South Central Nebraska, it nonetheless marked the sixth consecutive month of below normal precipitation across the majority of the 24-county area. Therefore, the overall situation remained largely unchanged as the summer and fall of 2012 continued marking its place in history as one of the worst droughts in many decades. Throughout the month, all 24 counties remained under a federally-designated drought disaster, and according to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor there was essentially no change whatsoever to drought classification. As a result, category D4 exceptional drought remained firmly in place across all or part of 15 counties, primarily those north of Interstate 80 and also west of Highway 183. Meanwhile, category D3 extreme drought persisted across most of the other nine counties, while only a small zone of category D2 severe drought held on in portions of Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, at least 85% of the area measured below normal precipitation in October. More significantly, roughly half of the area observed less than half of normal October precipitation, and roughly one-fifth of the area measured less than one-fourth of normal October precipitation. Focusing on actual precipitation totals, the majority of South Central Nebraska tallied between 0.50-1.75 inches. Mirroring trends from the past several months, the overall driest conditions remained anchored within the northwest half of the area. Per cooperative observers and a handful of automated stations, a few of the paltriest monthly precipitation totals included 0.17 at Greeley, 0.19 at Arcadia, 0.22 eight miles south of Elwood and 0.29 at Miller. In contrast, a few of the highest totals from eastern counties included 3.00 at Hebron, 2.20 at Bruning and 2.06 at Osceola. The majority of October rain was associated with thunderstorms on the 13th and 14th. ||Over the longer range period from May through-mid October, most of South Central Nebraska only realized between 20-60 percent of normal precipitation. Far northwest counties were the driest of the dry over this time span, highlighted by Ord with 3.67, merely 21% of the normal amount of 17.73. For the week ending October 14th, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that 97 percent of the state's pasture and rangeland was in poor to very poor condition. State-averaged subsoil and topsoil moisture rated 98 percent and 95 percent short to very short, respectively. Because of dry soils, less than half of the winter wheat crop had emerged, nearly two weeks behind average. Statewide corn harvest was 80 percent complete, over one month ahead of average, and soybean harvest was also well ahead of average at 86 percent complete.

Meanwhile, category D3 extreme drought persisted across most of the other nine counties, while only a small zone of category D2 severe drought held on in portions of Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, at least 85% of the area measured below normal precipitation in October. More significantly, roughly half of the area observed less than half of normal October precipitation, and roughly one-fifth of the area measured less than one-fourth of normal October precipitation. Focusing on actual precipitation totals, the majority of South Central Nebraska tallied between 0.50-1.75 inches. Mirroring trends from the past several months, the overall driest conditions remained anchored within the northwest half of the area. Per cooperative observers and a handful of automated stations, a few of the paltriest monthly precipitation totals included 0.17 at Greeley, 0.19 at Arcadia, 0.22 eight miles south of Elwood and 0.29 at Miller. In contrast, a few of the highest totals from eastern counties included 3.00 at Hebron, 2.20 at Bruning and 2.06 at Osceola. The majority of October rain was associated with thunderstorms on the 13th and 14th. ||Over the longer range period from May through-mid October, most of South Central Nebraska only realized between 20-60 percent of normal precipitation. Far northwest counties were the driest of the dry over this time span, highlighted by Ord with 3.67, merely 21% of the normal amount of 17.73. For the week ending October 14th, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that 97 percent of the state's pasture and rangeland was in poor to very poor condition. State-averaged subsoil and topsoil moisture rated 98 percent and 95 percent short to very short, respectively. Because of dry soils, less than half of the winter wheat crop had emerged, nearly two weeks behind average. Statewide corn harvest was 80 percent complete, over one month ahead of average, and soybean harvest was also well ahead of average at 86 percent complete.

Although October was not quite as dry as September across most of South Central Nebraska, it nonetheless marked the sixth consecutive month of below normal precipitation across the majority of the 24-county area. Therefore, the overall situation remained largely unchanged as the summer and fall of 2012 continued marking its place in history as one of the worst droughts in many decades. Throughout the month, all 24 counties remained under a federally-designated drought disaster, and according to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor there was essentially no change whatsoever to drought classification. As a result, category D4 exceptional drought remained firmly in place across all or part of 15 counties, primarily those north of Interstate 80 and also west of Highway 183. Meanwhile, category D3 extreme drought persisted across most of the other nine counties, while only a small zone of category D2 severe drought held on in portions of Nuckolls and Thayer counties. Based on reports from around 50 NWS cooperative observers, and also incorporating radar-derived estimation, at least 85% of the area measured below normal precipitation in October. More significantly, roughly half of the area observed less than half of normal October precipitation, and roughly one-fifth of the area measured less than one-fourth of normal October precipitation. Focusing on actual precipitation totals, the majority of South Central Nebraska tallied between 0.50-1.75 inches. Mirroring trends from the past several months, the overall driest conditions remained anchored within the northwest half of the area. Per cooperative observers and a handful of automated stations, a few of the paltriest monthly precipitation totals included 0.17 at Greeley, 0.19 at Arcadia, 0.22 eight miles south of Elwood and 0.29 at Miller. In contrast, a few of the highest totals from eastern counties included 3.00 at Hebron, 2.20 at Bruning and 2.06 at Osceola. The majority of October rain was associated with thunderstorms on the 13th and 14th. ||Over the longer range period from May through-mid October, most of South Central Nebraska only realized between 20-60 percent of normal precipitation. Far northwest counties were the driest of the dry over this time span, highlighted by Ord with 3.67, merely 21% of the normal amount of 17.73. For the week ending October 14th, the Nebraska Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that 97 percent of the state's pasture and rangeland was in poor to very poor condition. State-averaged subsoil and topsoil moisture rated 98 percent and 95 percent short to very short, respectively. Because of dry soils, less than half of the winter wheat crop had emerged, nearly two weeks behind average. Statewide corn harvest was 80 percent complete, over one month ahead of average, and soybean harvest was also well ahead of average at 86 percent complete.

Starting on Wednesday the 17th and then peaking on Thursday the 18th, South Central Nebraska experienced its overall windiest two-day period in several years. Blowing out of the northwest on both days, at least minor visibility restrictions due to blowing dust were common, and a few wildfires were sparked including a rather large one on the 17th in western Fillmore County. ||Focusing on the 18th, this was the windiest of the two days, and featured several hours of sustained speeds generally 35-45 MPH and frequent gusts of at least 50-60 MPH. The highest measured wind gust of the day at automated airport sites, 70 MPH, occurred at Evelyn Sharp Field in Ord, with a few other peak gusts at airports including 63 MPH at Grand Island, 60 MPH at Lexington and 59 MPH at Hastings, Aurora and York. While at least minor damage was likely widespread across the area, known damage reports included a trailer home with half its roof torn off in Cozad and a large tree branch that fell onto a moving car in Hastings. These intense winds were driven by deep mixing within an impressive surface pressure gradient between a 988 millibar low in northern Minnesota and a 1029 millibar high centered over Idaho. Attesting to the strength of this surface pressure gradient, late morning observations revealed a 20-millibar gradient across the state of Nebraska alone, ranging from 1016mb in the far southwest corner to 996mb in the far northeast.  This high wind event got underway unusually early in the day for northern parts of the area, as the Ord airport already gusted to 58 MPH prior to 7 AM CDT. Although sustained speeds largely eased below 40 MPH by 5 PM CDT, it remained quite windy well into the night with sustained speeds commonly 30+ MPH and gusts still around 40 MPH or higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearby AWOS recorded sustained wind speeds near and in excess of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought, which began in June, continued to intensify through September as another month of below normal precipitation was observed across western and north central Nebraska. A few significant storm systems did move across the central plains during the month, but produced very little rainfall across the area.  The drought caused a large fluctuations in temperatures with high temperatures being above normal, while low temperatures dropped to below normal values much of the month.  By the end of September, the Drought Monitor had expanded the area under Exceptional Drought (D4) across all of western and north central Nebraska as most places were experiencing a precipitation deficit in excess of eight inches for the year.  Affects of the drought included below normal to near record low flows on many of the rivers and streams across the area, damaged crops and pastures, lowered ground water levels leading to shallow wells drying up, as well as extreme fire danger.  In addition, the drought increased irrigation needs, which led to higher flows along the North Platte River which quickly depleted the water storage at Lake McConaughy.  The abundant dry air caused thunderstorms to produce little or no rainfall, which increased the threat for lightning strike fires.  Many grass fires were observed across the area, and while most were small, several larger fires occurred, requiring higher amounts of resources.  The drought continued into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought began in June and continued to intensify through September as below normal precipitation was observed across Custer County. For the month, rainfall was 1 to 3 inches below normal, while the yearly precipitation deficit was in excess of 8 inches for most locations. Custer County remained in Exceptional (D4) Drought going into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms affected only a small fraction of South Central Nebraska on this late Tuesday afternoon and early evening, but caused sporadic wind damage in portions of Nuckolls and Thayer Counties as outflow surged north from a complex of storms centered on and especially south of the state line in far northern Kansas. These strong southerly winds occurred mainly between 7 PM and 745 PM CDT, with reports including substantial roof damage to a two-story apartment building in downtown Superior and the sides blown off a golf cart shed under construction at the Hebron golf course. A few hours prior to this wind event and over 100 miles to the northwest, a completely separate, isolated storm flared up over southern Valley County around 430 PM CDT and strengthened as it drifted east, producing hail to around penny size north of Scotia before weakening. The initial development of high-based multicell storms that eventually produced the damaging winds near the state line occurred over extreme northwest Kansas during the mid-afternoon hours along a surface trough extending southwestward from north central Nebraska. This early activity then morphed into an increasingly organized quasi-linear complex as it charged east into far north central Kansas. In the mid and upper levels, flow was quasi-zonal and modest in strength, but contained weak embedded shortwave troughs well to the south of a much stronger wave sliding through eastern Montana. Although 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear was only around 30 knots, surface-based CAPE up to around 1000 J/kg in the presence of a hot and deeply-mixed airmass with surface temperatures around 100 F and dewpoints only in the 50s created a favorable environment for damaging outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 65 mph caused roof damage to a two story apartment building in the downtown area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly between 8 PM and 1 AM CDT on this Saturday evening and early Sunday morning, a few rounds of strong multicell storms rumbled through areas primarily between Interstate 80 and the Kansas border. Although radar data suggested that some of these storms might have contained marginally severe hail to around nickel-quarter size, the only known report of sizeable hail consisted of penny size stones in far north central Phelps County. The first round of convection affected western parts of the area, first developing over Dawson and far western Buffalo Counties around 8 PM CDT before drifting south primarily through Phelps, Harlan and Franklin Counties. Around 1030 PM CDT, a new and fairly widespread coverage of strong storms flared up farther east, primarily impacting Webster, Nuckolls, Fillmore and Thayer Counties before weakening and gradually fading away after midnight. Rainfall totals were generally less than one inch, although one of the higher exceptions included 1.43 in Hebron. This event was driven by a rather deep mid-upper level trough that lumbered directly over the Central Plains from west to east. Although the primary corridor of strong to severe storms during the late afternoon hours passed just southeast of the local area within a zone of stronger deep-layer wind shear, lingering most-unstable CAPE on the order of 500-1000 J/kg along with pronounced mid level convergence within the passing upper trough axis sparked the evening activity.

Over the course of about four hours, 12 rural fire departments battled this large wildfire that originated in western Fillmore County approximately four miles southeast of Sutton. The fire burned an area about three-quarters of a mile wide and 5 miles long in a southeast direction, encompassing about 3,500 acres of farmland as very strong northwest winds quickly intensified the fire. A total of two unoccupied homes and seven outbuildings were also destroyed, including 3 machine sheds and a barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large wildfire encompassing about 3500 acres of farmland erupted in western Fillmore County, approximately 4 miles southeast of Sutton, as very windy conditions quickly intensified the fire. The fire burned an area about three-quarters of a mile wide and 5 miles long, in a southeast direction. Several outbuildings were engulfed in flames.  A total of 2 unoccupied homes and 7 outbuildings were destroyed, including 3 machine sheds and a barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front raced south during the evening hours producing widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph. As the front passed through Benkelman, power lines were downed east of town which resulted in a small fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported two power poles were broken by strong winds with passage of a cold front.  Arcing power lines then started a small fire which burned about 40 acres east of Benkelman.  Time of report and peak wind gust estimated from Doppler radar and nearby mesonet observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 5-7 PM CDT on this early Thursday evening, a lone supercell storm dropped hail ranging from nickel to golf ball size along a roughly 35-mile path extending from just north of Orleans and Alma to just south of Franklin before departing southward into Smith County Kansas. The largest hail up to golf ball size was reported two miles south of Franklin. The first attempts at convective initiation got underway as early as 330 PM CDT near the Furnas-Harlan county line, but it took roughly 90 more minutes before a storm finally became established and started tracking east-southeast. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was driven by the invasion of a subtle shortwave trough embedded in seasonably strong quasi-zonal flow, well south of a primary mid level trough axis extending from Ontario to Montana. At the surface, this storm flared up along a rather well defined pre-frontal trough/dryline extending southward from north central Nebraska. This boundary marked a sharp moisture gradient, separating dewpoints in the 20s-30s F to the west from dewpoints in the upper 50s-lower 60s to the east. Late afternoon mesoscale severe weather parameters in far South Central Nebraska were supportive of supercells, featuring around 1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 40 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear.

Drought conditions continued from September with all of southwest Nebraska rated as being in Exceptional (D4) Drought.  Year-to-date precipitation through the end of October averaged 50% to 90% of normal.

Starting on Wednesday the 17th and then peaking on Thursday the 18th, South Central Nebraska experienced its overall windiest two-day period in several years. Blowing out of the northwest on both days, at least minor visibility restrictions due to blowing dust were common, and a few wildfires were sparked including a rather large one on the 17th in western Fillmore County. ||Focusing on the 18th, this was the windiest of the two days, and featured several hours of sustained speeds generally 35-45 MPH and frequent gusts of at least 50-60 MPH. The highest measured wind gust of the day at automated airport sites, 70 MPH, occurred at Evelyn Sharp Field in Ord, with a few other peak gusts at airports including 63 MPH at Grand Island, 60 MPH at Lexington and 59 MPH at Hastings, Aurora and York. While at least minor damage was likely widespread across the area, known damage reports included a trailer home with half its roof torn off in Cozad and a large tree branch that fell onto a moving car in Hastings. These intense winds were driven by deep mixing within an impressive surface pressure gradient between a 988 millibar low in northern Minnesota and a 1029 millibar high centered over Idaho. Attesting to the strength of this surface pressure gradient, late morning observations revealed a 20-millibar gradient across the state of Nebraska alone, ranging from 1016mb in the far southwest corner to 996mb in the far northeast.  This high wind event got underway unusually early in the day for northern parts of the area, as the Ord airport already gusted to 58 MPH prior to 7 AM CDT. Although sustained speeds largely eased below 40 MPH by 5 PM CDT, it remained quite windy well into the night with sustained speeds commonly 30+ MPH and gusts still around 40 MPH or higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained wind speeds of 40 to 48 mph occurred continuously between 1055 am CST and 1255 pm CST at the Lexington airport. Also of note, wind speeds of greater than 40 mph occurred at least periodically from 915 am CST to 1555 pm CST. A peak wind gust of 60 mph occurred at the Lexington Airport at 1135 CST. Also, a peak wind gust of 64 mph occurred at a mesonet site in Gothenburg, a 61 mph peak wind gust occured at a mesonet site three miles southeast of Overton, and a 58 mph peak wind gust occurred at a mesonet site nine miles north northwest of Lexington at 1235 CST. The exact time of peak wind gusts is unavailable from the mesonet sites. Also, a trailer home in Cozad had half of its roof torn off from non-thunderstorm gradient wind. A power pole in town was also damaged, and power lines were down west of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms affected only a small fraction of South Central Nebraska on this late Tuesday afternoon and early evening, but caused sporadic wind damage in portions of Nuckolls and Thayer Counties as outflow surged north from a complex of storms centered on and especially south of the state line in far northern Kansas. These strong southerly winds occurred mainly between 7 PM and 745 PM CDT, with reports including substantial roof damage to a two-story apartment building in downtown Superior and the sides blown off a golf cart shed under construction at the Hebron golf course. A few hours prior to this wind event and over 100 miles to the northwest, a completely separate, isolated storm flared up over southern Valley County around 430 PM CDT and strengthened as it drifted east, producing hail to around penny size north of Scotia before weakening. The initial development of high-based multicell storms that eventually produced the damaging winds near the state line occurred over extreme northwest Kansas during the mid-afternoon hours along a surface trough extending southwestward from north central Nebraska. This early activity then morphed into an increasingly organized quasi-linear complex as it charged east into far north central Kansas. In the mid and upper levels, flow was quasi-zonal and modest in strength, but contained weak embedded shortwave troughs well to the south of a much stronger wave sliding through eastern Montana. Although 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear was only around 30 knots, surface-based CAPE up to around 1000 J/kg in the presence of a hot and deeply-mixed airmass with surface temperatures around 100 F and dewpoints only in the 50s created a favorable environment for damaging outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated near 65 mph caused damage to the sides of a golf cart shed that was under construction.

Although a few smaller fires were reported within the 24-county area on this day, by far the largest wildfire erupted in western Fillmore County near Sutton shortly after 3 PM CDT. Over the course of about four hours, 12 rural fire departments battled this fire as it consumed a corridor of farmland estimated at 3,500 acres along with several outbuildings and two unoccupied homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 61 mph occurred at the Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island, NE.

Attesting to the strength of this surface pressure gradient, late morning observations revealed a 20-millibar gradient across the state of Nebraska alone, ranging from 1016mb in the far southwest corner to 996mb in the far northeast.  This high wind event got underway unusually early in the day for northern parts of the area, as the Ord airport already gusted to 58 MPH prior to 7 AM CDT. Although sustained speeds largely eased below 40 MPH by 5 PM CDT, it remained quite windy well into the night with sustained speeds commonly 30+ MPH and gusts still around 40 MPH or higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearby ASOS recorded sustained wind speeds near and in excess of 40 mph and numerous gusts over 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained over much of eastern Nebraska into November as precipitation continued below normal, generally a half an inch or less (although a little over 1 inch fell over parts of far southeast Nebraska).  These amounts were around an inch below normal.  Thus, Exceptional Drought conditions continued in northeast Nebraska near and northwest of a line from Columbus to Pender with the Drought intensity remaining in the Extreme category southeast of there from around Lincoln and Beatrice through Omaha.  Conditions were slightly better southeast of Lincoln and Omaha, but they were still in the Severe category.  Extreme or Exceptional drought conditions persisted over much of the area into December.

Starting on Wednesday the 17th and then peaking on Thursday the 18th, South Central Nebraska experienced its overall windiest two-day period in several years. Blowing out of the northwest on both days, at least minor visibility restrictions due to blowing dust were common, and a few wildfires were sparked including a rather large one on the 17th in western Fillmore County. ||Focusing on the 18th, this was the windiest of the two days, and featured several hours of sustained speeds generally 35-45 MPH and frequent gusts of at least 50-60 MPH. The highest measured wind gust of the day at automated airport sites, 70 MPH, occurred at Evelyn Sharp Field in Ord, with a few other peak gusts at airports including 63 MPH at Grand Island, 60 MPH at Lexington and 59 MPH at Hastings, Aurora and York. While at least minor damage was likely widespread across the area, known damage reports included a trailer home with half its roof torn off in Cozad and a large tree branch that fell onto a moving car in Hastings. These intense winds were driven by deep mixing within an impressive surface pressure gradient between a 988 millibar low in northern Minnesota and a 1029 millibar high centered over Idaho. Attesting to the strength of this surface pressure gradient, late morning observations revealed a 20-millibar gradient across the state of Nebraska alone, ranging from 1016mb in the far southwest corner to 996mb in the far northeast.  This high wind event got underway unusually early in the day for northern parts of the area, as the Ord airport already gusted to 58 MPH prior to 7 AM CDT. Although sustained speeds largely eased below 40 MPH by 5 PM CDT, it remained quite windy well into the night with sustained speeds commonly 30+ MPH and gusts still around 40 MPH or higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearby ASOS recorded sustained wind speeds near and in excess of 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms affected only a small fraction of South Central Nebraska on this late Tuesday afternoon and early evening, but caused sporadic wind damage in portions of Nuckolls and Thayer Counties as outflow surged north from a complex of storms centered on and especially south of the state line in far northern Kansas. These strong southerly winds occurred mainly between 7 PM and 745 PM CDT, with reports including substantial roof damage to a two-story apartment building in downtown Superior and the sides blown off a golf cart shed under construction at the Hebron golf course. A few hours prior to this wind event and over 100 miles to the northwest, a completely separate, isolated storm flared up over southern Valley County around 430 PM CDT and strengthened as it drifted east, producing hail to around penny size north of Scotia before weakening. The initial development of high-based multicell storms that eventually produced the damaging winds near the state line occurred over extreme northwest Kansas during the mid-afternoon hours along a surface trough extending southwestward from north central Nebraska. This early activity then morphed into an increasingly organized quasi-linear complex as it charged east into far north central Kansas. In the mid and upper levels, flow was quasi-zonal and modest in strength, but contained weak embedded shortwave troughs well to the south of a much stronger wave sliding through eastern Montana. Although 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear was only around 30 knots, surface-based CAPE up to around 1000 J/kg in the presence of a hot and deeply-mixed airmass with surface temperatures around 100 F and dewpoints only in the 50s created a favorable environment for damaging outflow winds.

Between 5-7 PM CDT on this early Thursday evening, a lone supercell storm dropped hail ranging from nickel to golf ball size along a roughly 35-mile path extending from just north of Orleans and Alma to just south of Franklin before departing southward into Smith County Kansas. The largest hail up to golf ball size was reported two miles south of Franklin. The first attempts at convective initiation got underway as early as 330 PM CDT near the Furnas-Harlan county line, but it took roughly 90 more minutes before a storm finally became established and started tracking east-southeast. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was driven by the invasion of a subtle shortwave trough embedded in seasonably strong quasi-zonal flow, well south of a primary mid level trough axis extending from Ontario to Montana. At the surface, this storm flared up along a rather well defined pre-frontal trough/dryline extending southward from north central Nebraska. This boundary marked a sharp moisture gradient, separating dewpoints in the 20s-30s F to the west from dewpoints in the upper 50s-lower 60s to the east. Late afternoon mesoscale severe weather parameters in far South Central Nebraska were supportive of supercells, featuring around 1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 40 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear.

After more than three weeks void of strong thunderstorms anywhere in South Central Nebraska, this Sunday evening featured a cluster of strong to marginally severe storms that drifted south through far western portions of the area, primarily affecting Dawson, Gosper and Furnas counties. The strongest of these multicell storms impacted western Dawson County as it shifted south out of Custer County between 630-730 PM CDT, dropping quarter size hail near Cozad. This storm weakened below severe limits as it moved south of Interstate 80. Although a few other strong storms briefly flared up across parts of Gosper and Furnas counties during the ensuing few hours, none of these became severe, and all thunderstorms exited the area into northwest Kansas by 945 PM CDT. This activity focused along a well-defined surface trough, which also defined a narrow north-south instability axis where surface dewpoints pooled into the low-mid 50s F. The flow aloft in the mid-upper levels was northerly, and contained weak embedded shortwave troughs in between two much stronger disturbances, one centered near the OK/AR border and the other diving out of Canada into the Northern Plains. Around the time of the quarter size hail near Cozad, mesoscale parameters were marginally supportive of severe storms, featuring around 1000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE but only 25 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear.

Record setting heat and dryness intensified drought conditions over eastern Nebraska during July.  Rainfall which was already deficient for the season going into July, became even more sparse during the month with many sites recording less than 1/2 inch and some, including Omaha, barely measuring.  In addition, high temperatures consistently rose above 95 degrees with many sites recording at least 5 days with highs above 100 in July.  The persistent heat and dry weather caused many rivers to drop to very low flows, including the Platte River which dried up completely upstream of the Loup River.  The drought also caused some local wells to dry up or go to very low flow rates.  The low water prompted the Nebraska Dept. of Natural Resources to institute irrigation halts for selective waterways including the Loup and Big Blue Rivers.  The low river levels and irrigation pressure from underground water sources also caused some municipalities to begin voluntary or mandatory water restrictions.  According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, drought conditions reached the Severe intensity on July 17th in northeast Nebraska roughly along and northwest of a line from Columbus to Wayne.   The continued hot and dry weather pushed that region into the Extreme category between July 24th and 31st and brought at least Severe drought conditions to the rest of eastern Nebraska by the 24th of July.  Extreme drought conditions spread east to a line from near Schuyler to Pender by the end of the month.  The drought in Nebraska and surrounding states caused some crop prices to reach record or near record levels in July and some dry-land corn that failed to produce kernels was baled or cut as silage.  By the end of the month some 38 percent of the soybean and corn crop was considered poor or very poor by the USDA.  The Severe to Extreme drought continued and intensified into August.
